The camera will erase all images that are not protected. If any images have been protected, they will remain on the CF card. To delete protected images too, you would need to cancel protection first, or format the CF card if you want to completely clear the card, including protected images.,['Camera_58', 'Camera_59', 'Manual10_187']

To print photos from the camera using the CP Direct method, follow these steps:

1. Make sure you are using a CP Direct-compatible printer and the correct cable.
   - If the printer supports PictBridge as well, use the interface cable supplied with the camera.
   - If the printer is only compatible with CP Direct, use the cable supplied with the printer.

<PIC>

2. Connect the cable to the camera’s DIGITAL terminal.
   Insert the plug carefully, with the plug icon facing the front side of the camera. Then connect the other end to the printer.

<PIC>

3. Turn on the printer, then turn on the camera.

4. Press the Playback button on the camera to display the photos on the LCD monitor.

5. Confirm that the CP Direct connection icon appears in the upper-left corner of the LCD monitor. This confirms the camera has recognized the printer through CP Direct.

<PIC>

6. Select the photo you want to print.
   Turn the camera’s main dial to browse through the images on the CF card. When the desired photo is displayed, press the SET button to open the print setting screen.

7. Adjust the print options as needed.
   Depending on the printer and paper loaded, you may be able to set options such as the number of copies, print style/layout, date imprinting, or trimming. Use the dial to choose an item, press SET to change it, then confirm the setting.

8. Start printing.
   On the print setting screen, select [Print], then press SET. Printing will begin. When printing finishes, the camera returns to the image display/print selection screen so you can choose another photo if needed.

Additional tips:
- If you are powering the camera with a battery, make sure it is fully charged before printing.
- To stop printing, press SET while [Stop] is displayed, select [OK], then press SET again.
- If an error message appears on the camera’s LCD monitor, resolve the printer issue first, then continue or restart printing.
- Before disconnecting the cable, turn off both the camera and printer. Remove the cable by holding the plug, not by pulling the cord.,['Manual10_191', 'Manual10_192', 'Manual10_193']

To fine-tune the camera for comfortable viewing while using it, adjust the viewfinder diopter. This lets you match the viewfinder to your eyesight so the AF points and viewfinder information look sharp.

1. Look through the viewfinder.
2. Aim the camera at a bright, plain subject, such as a wall or the sky. Do not look at the sun or any very bright light source.
3. Turn the dioptric adjustment knob left or right.
4. Stop when the AF points in the viewfinder look crisp and clear. The subject itself does not need to be perfectly focused for this adjustment—the key is that the AF points/viewfinder markings look sharp.

<PIC>

The knob is normally set around the standard position of -1 dpt. If you turn the knob through its full range and the viewfinder still does not look sharp, you may need an optional Dioptric Adjustment Lens E that better matches your eyesight.,['Manual10_54']

After you receive the Active Noise Canceling Truly Wireless Earphones, please check that you have and can identify the following main parts:

1. Complete earphone set
You should have the pair of wireless earbuds and the charging case. The earbuds sit inside the case when charging or being stored.

<PIC>

2. Multifunction buttons on the earbuds
Each earbud has a multifunction button on the outer face. This is used for key controls such as music playback, calls, pairing-related actions, and other functions.

<PIC>

3. Left and right earbuds
There is one Left Earbud and one Right Earbud. You can identify them by the L/R markings on the earbuds. The inner side also has charging contacts that connect with the charging case.

<PIC>

4. Status LED indicators and charging case battery LEDs
Each earbud has a status LED indicator that shows earbud status, such as charging, pairing, or connection status. The charging case has battery level LED indicators on the front, which show the remaining case battery level.

<PIC>

5. Battery level display button on the charging case
The charging case also has a battery level display button. Pressing this button lets you check the charging case battery level using the LED indicators.

<PIC>

In short, the key components to confirm are: the left earbud, the right earbud, the charging case, the multifunction buttons, the earbud status LEDs, the case battery level LEDs, and the case battery level display button. If any main part appears to be missing or damaged, please let us know and we’ll be happy to help.,['Manual12_0', 'Manual12_1', 'Manual12_2', 'Manual12_3', 'Manual12_4']

If the charging case battery is low, you can recharge it with the USB-C charging cable as follows:

1. Plug the USB-C end of the cable into the charging port on the back of the charging case. Make sure it is inserted gently and securely.
<PIC>

2. Connect the other, standard USB end of the cable to a suitable USB power source, such as a USB wall charger, computer USB port, or power bank.
<PIC>

3. After it starts charging, the Battery Level LED Indicators on the case will show the current charging level:
- 0–24%: 1 blue LED flashes
- 25–49%: 1 solid blue LED + 1 flashing blue LED
- 50–74%: 2 solid blue LEDs + 1 flashing blue LED
- 75–99%: 3 solid blue LEDs + 1 flashing blue LED
- 100%: 4 solid blue LEDs stay on
<PIC>

4. You can also check the case battery level at any time by pressing the Battery Level Display Button on the right side of the case.
<PIC>

For best results, keep the charging cable and case in a well-ventilated area while charging, and avoid charging if the case or earbuds are wet.,['earphones_04', 'earphones_05', 'earphones_06', 'earphones_07']

When pairing and connecting your earbuds via Bluetooth, you may encounter these common scenarios:

1. First-time pairing after opening the charging case
When you open the charging case, the two earbuds will first connect to each other automatically. After that, they will enter Bluetooth pairing mode so your phone, tablet, or computer can find them.

While the earbuds are still inside the case, the earbud LEDs show charging/battery status instead of Bluetooth status:
- Solid red: low battery
- Flashing light blue: charging
- Solid blue: fully charged

<PIC>

2. Pairing or connected status after removing the earbuds
Once you take the earbuds out of the case, the LEDs indicate Bluetooth status:
- Flashing light blue: the earbuds are in pairing mode and waiting to connect
- Solid light blue: the earbuds are connected to a device

<PIC>

3. Connecting from your device’s Bluetooth settings
On your phone or other Bluetooth device, open the Bluetooth settings page and make sure Bluetooth is turned ON. Then look under available devices and select the earbuds. The device name may appear as “AL Hydrabuds ANC” on the Bluetooth list.

<PIC>

4. Automatic reconnection
If the earbuds were previously paired with a nearby device, they may automatically reconnect to that device when powered on. In this case, they may not appear as available for pairing on a new device until you disconnect them from the previously connected device or turn Bluetooth off on that device.

5. Pairing to a new device
If you want to pair the earbuds with a different phone, tablet, or computer, turn Bluetooth OFF on the device currently connected. The earbuds should return to pairing mode. You can then select them from the Bluetooth list on the new device.

Alternatively, you can press, then press and hold either earbud to drop the current connection and enter pairing mode.

6. Multipoint connection with two devices
The earbuds support multipoint Bluetooth connection, which allows connection with up to two devices, such as a smartphone and a computer.

To set this up:
- Pair the earbuds with the first device.
- Turn Bluetooth OFF on the first device so the earbuds become available for pairing again.
- Pair the earbuds with the second device.
- Turn Bluetooth back ON on the first device. The earbuds should automatically reconnect to it.

After this, both devices are paired, and you can use either one for music or calls.

7. If connection does not work as expected
If the earbuds do not appear in the Bluetooth list, connect to the wrong device, or fail to pair, please try the following:
- Make sure the earbuds are charged.
- Keep the earbuds close to your phone or device.
- Turn Bluetooth OFF and ON again on your device.
- Remove/forget the earbuds from the Bluetooth list, then pair again.
- Turn Bluetooth off on nearby previously paired devices to prevent automatic reconnection.
- If the earbuds become unresponsive, a reset may be needed.,['Manual12_5', 'Manual12_6', 'Manual12_7']

The two main controlling functions of the earphones are music control and call control. You can use the multifunction buttons on the left or right earbud to manage both functions directly from the earphones.

1. Controlling Music
You can play or pause music, adjust the volume, and switch tracks using different press actions on the earbuds:
• Press either earbud once: Play/Pause
• Press the left earbud 2 times: Volume up
• Press the right earbud 2 times: Volume down
• Press the left earbud 3 times: Previous track
• Press the right earbud 3 times: Next track


2. Controlling Calls
You can also manage phone calls from the earbuds without using your phone:
• Press either earbud once: Answer a call
• Press and hold either earbud: End a call
• Press and hold either earbud when a call is incoming: Reject the call
,['Manual12_8', 'Manual12_9']

Besides the two main functions—controlling music playback and managing calls—you can also operate the following earphone features using the multifunction touch buttons:

1. Voice Assistant
Press and hold the left earbud until you hear the first beep, then release. This will activate your phone’s voice assistant.
<PIC>

2. Music App Shortcut
Press and hold the right earbud until you hear the first beep, then release. This will open or resume your music app from where you left off. If there was no previous playback session, you can tap again and the app may start playing recommended music.
<PIC>

3. Ambient Awareness / ANC Modes
Press the left earbud to cycle through Ambient Awareness and ANC modes, allowing you to switch between hearing more of your surroundings or reducing outside noise.
<PIC>

4. Low Latency Mode
Press and hold either earbud until you hear the second beep, then release. Low Latency Mode helps reduce Bluetooth audio delay, which is especially useful for gaming and better lip-sync when watching videos.
<PIC>,['earphones_01', 'earphones_02', 'earphones_03', 'Manual12_10']

If your earphones are not working properly, you can reset them using the multifunction buttons on both earbuds. Before resetting, please first disconnect the earphones from Bluetooth and remove both earbuds from the charging case.

1. Factory reset
Use this if you want to clear the earphones’ saved settings and pairing information.

Press and hold the multifunction button on both the left and right earbuds at the same time for about 6 seconds. The earbuds should show flashing red and blue lights during the reset.

<PIC>

After the factory reset, place the earbuds back in the charging case briefly, then take them out again. They should enter pairing mode. Go to your phone’s Bluetooth settings, remove/forget the old earphones connection if it is still listed, then pair with the earphones again.

2. Hardware reset
Use this only if the earbuds are locked up, frozen, or completely unresponsive.

Press and hold the multifunction button on both earbuds at the same time for about 10 seconds. This restarts the earphones.

<PIC>

After the hardware reset, press the multifunction button again to power the earbuds back on. If they still do not connect, put them back into the charging case, make sure the case and earbuds have enough charge, then remove them and try pairing again.,['Manual12_11', 'Manual12_12']

To keep your Active Noise Canceling Truly Wireless Earphones in good condition, please follow these maintenance and care guidelines:

1. Cleaning
- Wipe the earphones and charging case gently with a soft cloth or paper towel.
- Do not use harsh chemicals, detergents, sprays, or abrasive cleaners.
- Make sure the earphones and charging contacts are completely dry before placing them in the charging case or charging the case.

<PIC>

2. Keep them dry and protected
- Avoid using or storing the earphones near water, on wet surfaces, in rain, or where they may be exposed to liquid sprays.
- Although the earphones are rated IP55 for sweat and dust resistance, they should not be submerged in water or charged while wet.

3. Storage
- When not in use, store the earphones in the charging case and keep them in a cool, dry place.
- Keep them away from direct sunlight, excessive heat, fire, and extreme cold.
- Do not expose the product to temperatures above 100°F.

4. Charging and cable care
- Connect and disconnect the charging cable carefully.
- Do not tug, yank, or pull on the cable while it is connected to the charging case.
- While charging, keep the cable well ventilated and away from flammable materials such as bedding, linens, or synthetic fabrics.

5. Handling precautions
- Do not drop, crush, or expose the earphones or charging case to strong physical impact.
- Do not attempt to repair, modify, or take apart the product yourself. If service is needed, please contact a qualified service center.
- Keep the earphones away from children, as small parts may present a choking hazard.

6. Disposal and recycling
- Please recycle or dispose of the earphones properly according to your local laws and regulations.
- For more details, you may contact your local recycling facility or the manufacturer.,['Manual12_13']

From the three observing views, this eReader has the following main buttons, controls, and connection interfaces:

<PIC>

**Front view**

- **[1] Home / ESC button**: Cancels the current operation, exits a sub-interface, or returns to the previous screen.
- **[2] Previous / Next Page buttons**: Turns to the previous or next page while reading.
- **[3] Navigation / Menu control**: A 4-way navigation control used to move left, right, up, and down. The center **“M”** button works as the Menu / Confirm button.
- **[4] Zoom in / out**: Enlarges or reduces JPEG images, and can also adjust the font size while reading eBooks.
- **[5] Rotate button**: Rotates the eBook display between horizontal and vertical viewing.
- **[9] Play / Pause button**: Starts or pauses music playback or voice recording playback.
- **[10] Power button**: Press and hold for about 3–5 seconds to power the device on or off.
- **[15] TFT LCD display screen**: 7-inch diagonal color display.

The front view also shows the main reading and media-control layout, including page-turn buttons on the side and function buttons along the lower area.

<PIC>

**Navigation button view**

The navigation control is a circular 4-way button:

- **Up button**: Moves the selection upward. During music playback, it can also increase the volume.
- **Down button**: Moves the selection downward. During music playback, it can also decrease the volume.
- **Left button**: Moves the selection left or backward, depending on the interface.
- **Right button**: Moves the selection right or forward, depending on the interface.
- **Center “M” button**: Opens the menu, enters a selected item, or confirms an operation.

<PIC>

**Bottom view**

From left to right, the bottom side includes these switches and interfaces:

- **Power switch with OFF / ON positions**: Hardware power switch for enabling or disabling the device power.
- **MIC hole**: Built-in microphone opening for voice recording.
- **TF Card / Micro SD card slot**: Used for inserting a TF / Micro SD card. The device supports Micro SD / SDHC cards up to 16GB.
- **3.5 mm earphone jack**: Connects headphones or earphones for private listening.
- **Mini USB connector / USB port**: Used for charging the eReader and connecting it to a computer to transfer files.

So, in summary, the eReader includes front-side reading controls, a central 4-way navigation pad with an “M” confirm/menu button, and bottom-side interfaces for power switching, microphone input, TF/Micro SD storage expansion, earphones, and Mini USB connection.,['Manual13_0', 'Manual13_1', 'Manual13_2']

The eReader has two related but different functions: Main Menu is the starting point for all features, while Browser History helps you quickly reopen files you have recently read. 1. Main Menu The Main Menu shows all major functions available on the device. From this screen, you can choose what you want to do, such as reading books, playing music or videos, viewing photos, recording audio, managing files, or changing settings. The available options include: - Browser History: View recently opened reading files. - eBook: Open supported eBook files such as TXT, EPUB, PDF, FB2, and HTML. - Music: Play audio files. - Video: Play supported video files. - Photo: View pictures. - Record: Use voice recording functions. - Explorer: Browse and manage files stored on the device or Micro SD card. - Settings: Adjust device settings such as display, language, time, date, and system options. Use the Navigation keys to move between options, then press the “M” button to enter the selected function. Press the Home/ESC button to return to the previous screen. 2. Browser History The Browser History function displays the files you have recently opened or read. It is useful when you want to continue reading a book without searching for it again in the eBook list or file explorer. How it works: - Enter Browser History from the Main Menu. - Use the Navigation keys to select a recently opened book or document. - Press the “M” button to open it. - The device will automatically return to the last page you read in that selected file, so you can continue reading from where you stopped. Important note: If a recently opened file was stored on a Micro SD card and that file has been deleted or the card is removed, the record may still appear in Browser History. However, the file cannot be opened unless it is restored to the original storage location.,['Manual13_3', 'Manual13_4']

In eBook reading mode, pressing the “M” button will open the eBook function menu on the screen. From this menu, you can use the navigation keys to select an option, then press “M” again to confirm.

The menu includes these options:

- “Page Jump”: go directly to a specific page number.
- “Save Mark”: save the current page as a bookmark.
- “Load Mark”: open a previously saved bookmark.
- “Del Mark”: delete a saved bookmark.
- “Browser Mode”: switch between manual browsing and auto browsing.
- “Flip Time”: set the automatic page-turning interval, such as 10, 15, 20, 25, or 30 seconds.
- “Brightness”: adjust the screen brightness level.
- “Set Color”: change the text color for more comfortable reading.

<PIC>

So, pressing “M” in eBook mode does not turn the page directly; it brings up the reading options menu, where you can manage bookmarks, jump pages, and adjust reading settings.,['eReader_08']

To listen to music, open the audio files list in the main menu, select the desired audio file, then press "M" to play.

<PIC>

You can add audio files by connecting the eReader to your computer via USB cable, then drag your desired audio files and paste them into this device. This device supports MP3, WMA, WAC, ACC and ECT format.

<PIC>

You can also connect earphones to the 3.5 mm earphone jack for private enjoyment. Press the "Play / Pause" key to pause or continue play, and press the "Up/Down" key to adjust the volume.,['Manual13_3', 'Manual13_7']

Yes. This eReader supports voice recording.

To record voice:
1. From the main menu, select “Record”, then press the “M” button to enter voice record mode.
<PIC>

2. Press the “Play/Pause” button to start recording.
3. Press “Play/Pause” again to pause recording.
4. Press “HOME” to stop recording.
5. After recording, the device will ask whether to save the file. Select “YES” or “NO” by pressing the “M” key.
<PIC>

To play back a recording:

2. Select “Record” and press “M” to open the recorded file list.
3. Select the desired recorded file, then press “M” to start playback.,['Manual13_11', 'Manual13_12']

Yes, you can play videos on this eReader from the Video menu. Please follow these steps:

4. Press “M” again to open and start playing the selected video.

Supported video formats are: AVI, RMVB, and MPEG2. If your video does not appear or cannot be played, please check whether the file is in one of these formats. If needed, you can transfer compatible video files to the device by connecting it to your computer with a USB cable, then copying the video file into the device storage or Micro SD card.

While a video is playing, you can use these controls:
- Play/Pause: Press the Play/Pause button to pause or continue playback.
- Open video options: Press the “M” button during playback.
- Subtitle Language: If the video file includes subtitles, press “M” and choose “Subtitle Language” to change it.

If the video still will not play, the file may use an unsupported resolution or encoding even if the file extension looks correct. In that case, try converting it to AVI, RMVB, or MPEG2 and then copy it back to the eReader.,['Manual13_9']

Yes. This eReader can be used as a photo viewer, so you can browse photos stored on the device or on a Micro SD card.

2. Press the “M” button to enter Photo mode.

<PIC>

Once you are in Photo mode, you can operate it as follows:

Here are the most common issues you may encounter when using the eReader, along with practical steps to resolve them. The image below can help you identify the main buttons and ports mentioned in the troubleshooting steps, such as the Power button, USB port, Micro SD card slot, navigation/Menu button, and Reset switch.



1. The eReader will not power on

Possible causes:
- The battery is too low.
- The device has temporarily frozen.
- There may be a hardware fault.

What to do:
a. Connect the eReader to a computer or USB power source using the USB cable and charge it until the battery indicator is full.
b. Press and hold the Power button for about 3–5 seconds to turn it on.
c. If the screen does not respond after a full charge, try using the Reset switch on the back of the device, near the card reader.
d. If it still cannot power on after charging and resetting, please contact customer support for further help. Do not open or disassemble the device yourself.

2. The eReader does not recognize the Micro SD card

Possible causes:
- The card was inserted while the device was powered on.
- The card is not seated correctly.
- The card capacity or format may not be compatible.

What to do:
a. Power off the eReader completely before removing or inserting the Micro SD card.
b. Remove the card, check its direction, and insert it again firmly.
c. Power the device back on and check whether the card is detected.
d. Make sure the card is a Micro SD/SDHC card within the supported capacity, up to 16GB.
e. Important: Do not use the Reset switch while a Micro SD card is inserted, as this may cause card recognition or file issues.

3. Files cannot be downloaded or transferred to the eReader

Possible causes:
- The USB cable is damaged or only supports charging, not data transfer.
- The device memory is full.
- The computer does not recognize the eReader as a removable disk.
- The operating system is very old and may need a USB driver.

What to do:
a. Use a working USB data cable and connect the eReader to your computer.
b. After connection, the eReader should show “USB Connection,” and the computer should detect it as a removable disk.
c. If a Micro SD card is inserted, the computer may show two removable disks: one for internal memory and one for the card.
d. If the device is not detected, try another USB port, another USB cable, or restart both the computer and the eReader.
e. Check whether the internal memory or Micro SD card is full. Delete unused files or move files to another storage device.
f. If using an older system such as Windows 98, a USB 2.0 driver may be required.

4. Music files cannot be played

Possible causes:
- The music format is not supported.
- The file resolution/encoding is not compatible.
- The file may be damaged.

What to do:
a. Try playing another music file to confirm whether the issue is with one file or all music files.
b. Use common audio formats supported by most portable players.
c. If a specific file does not play, convert it to a more standard audio format and try again.
d. Make sure the file was fully transferred to the eReader and is not corrupted.
e. If no music files can be played, restart the device and test again.

5. The screen display is poor or the device responds slowly

Possible causes:
- Brightness or light time settings are not suitable.
- The device is being used outside the recommended temperature range.
- The system may be temporarily slow due to file loading.

What to do:
a. Go to Settings and adjust the display brightness to a comfortable level.
b. Avoid using the device in very cold, hot, humid, dusty, or high-temperature environments. The recommended operating temperature is 32–120°F / 0–50°C.
c. If the device becomes slow, restart it and avoid opening very large files repeatedly.
d. Keep the screen protected and avoid pressure, bending, dropping, or impact.

6. Buttons do not respond or the device seems locked

Possible causes:
- Key lock may be enabled.
- The device has temporarily frozen.

What to do:
a. Check the key lock time setting. You can set it to 10 seconds, 30 seconds, 5 minutes, 10 minutes, or No Locking.
b. If the device does not respond, press and hold the Power button to restart it.
c. If restarting does not help, use the Reset switch. Please remove any Micro SD card before resetting.

7. The eReader shuts down automatically

Possible causes:
- Auto shutdown time is enabled.
- Battery power is low.

What to do:
a. Check the automatic shutdown setting. Available options include 3, 5, 10, 15, and 20 minutes.
b. Select a longer time if you do not want the device to shut down quickly.
c. Charge the device if the battery is low.

8. The device still does not work properly after normal operation

Please stop using the device and contact customer support if any of the following happens:
- Liquid or foreign objects enter the device.
- The device gets wet from rain or water.
- The device is damaged after being dropped.
- The device becomes obviously overheated.
- The device still cannot work normally after charging, restarting, and resetting.

For safety, do not attempt to repair or disassemble the eReader yourself.,['Manual13_0']

To properly complete the fax connection, please finish the setup in this order:

1. Place the machine close to both a telephone wall jack and an easily accessible AC power outlet. Keep the cords arranged so they are not stretched, pinched, or in a walkway.

2. Connect the fax line using a standard telephone line cord. Use only a No. 26 AWG or larger telecommunication line cord, and connect it to a compatible modular telephone wall jack, such as USOC RJ11C. Make sure the plug clicks firmly into place.

3. Check the power rating label on the machine, then connect it only to a matching AC power source. Do not connect the machine to a DC power source or inverter.

4. If a power cord was provided with the product, use only that power cord. Insert the grounded plug into a grounded AC power outlet after confirming the local power supply rating is suitable.

<PIC>

5. After the telephone line and power cord are connected, keep the AC outlet clear and easy to reach. In an emergency, unplug the power cord from the AC outlet to shut off the power completely.

Important safety notes: avoid installing or changing telephone wiring during a thunderstorm, do not install a telephone wall jack in a wet location, and never touch uninsulated telephone wires or terminals unless the telephone line has been disconnected from the wall jack.,['Manual15_2']

To use the fax safely, please pay special attention to the points below. These precautions help reduce the risk of electric shock, fire, injury, or damage to the machine.

1. Place the fax in a safe and stable location

Put the fax on a flat, level, stable surface, such as a desk, where it will not be exposed to vibration or shocks. Keep it near a telephone wall jack and an easily accessible AC power outlet so you can unplug it quickly in an emergency.

Please avoid placing it on an unstable cart, stand, or table, especially where children may be nearby. Do not place heavy objects on top of the fax, and keep the area around the power outlet clear. Also make sure power cords, telephone cords, and other cables are secured so they do not create a tripping hazard.

For fax use, also leave enough space in front of the machine so received faxes or printed pages can come out smoothly without being blocked.

2. Prevent electrical shock hazards

If the fax has been dropped, the casing is damaged, liquid has entered the machine, or it becomes unusually hot, gives off smoke, or produces a strong smell, unplug it from the AC power outlet immediately and contact authorized service personnel.

Do not use the fax during a thunderstorm, as there may be a risk of electrical shock from lightning. Also, connect the fax only to a suitable AC power source within the rating shown on the machine. Do not connect it to a DC power source or inverter.

<PIC>

3. Avoid fire hazards when cleaning or using the fax

Do not use flammable substances, sprays, or cleaning liquids containing alcohol, ammonia, or organic solvents on the inside or outside of the fax. These may cause a fire or damage the product. For routine cleaning, use only a dry, lint-free cloth.

Also keep metal objects such as clips, staples, or jewelry away from the top and inside of the fax. If they fall into the machine and touch internal electrical parts, they may cause fire or electric shock.

<PIC>

4. Use the power cord correctly

Use only the power cord supplied with the fax, if one was provided. Do not place anything on the cord, do not position the machine where people may step on the cord, and do not stretch, pinch, or tightly bend the cord.

Do not use the fax if the power cord is frayed, cracked, or damaged. Do not pull the cord from the middle when unplugging it; hold the plug instead. It is also recommended not to use extension cords, as improper power connections may increase the risk of fire or electric shock.

5. Be careful with telephone lines

When installing or changing telephone lines, always handle them carefully. Never touch uninsulated telephone wires or terminals unless the telephone line has been unplugged from the wall jack.

Do not install telephone wiring during a thunderstorm, and do not install a telephone wall jack in a wet or damp location. To reduce risk when connecting or servicing the fax, disconnect cables from the wall outlet first.

6. Keep hands clear of trays, slots, and moving parts

Wait until pages have completely exited the fax before picking them up. Pulling pages too early may trap or injure your fingers.

Do not put your hand or any foreign object into the ink insertion slot or other openings. If the paper tray is extended for large-size paper, position the fax so the tray does not stick out beyond the edge of the desk or table. If someone bumps the extended tray, the fax could fall and cause injury.

<PIC>

Be especially careful around the scanner cover, document feeder, hinges, and shaded moving-part areas. Keep your fingers away from the areas shown below, particularly when opening or closing covers or when parts are moving.

<PIC>

7. Keep liquids, small objects, packaging, and ink away from children

Do not use the fax near water or in places that may become wet, such as near a sink, washing machine, wet basement, or swimming pool. If water, another liquid, or a metal object gets inside the fax, unplug it immediately and contact authorized service personnel.

Keep plastic bags from the packaging away from babies and children to avoid suffocation risk. Keep ink cartridges out of children’s reach, do not swallow ink, and avoid getting ink in your eyes or mouth. If ink contacts your skin, wash with soap and water. If it gets into your eyes or mouth, rinse immediately and seek medical advice.

8. Do not open or repair the fax yourself

Do not dismantle the fax or try to service internal parts yourself. Opening or removing covers may expose you to dangerous voltage points and other risks. If the fax needs repair, inspection, or internal service, please contact authorized service personnel.

9. Additional fax-specific precautions

Do not use the fax to report a gas leak while you are near the leak. If your fax model has wireless LAN and you use medical electrical equipment or a pacemaker, keep a safe distance if you notice any discomfort or abnormal operation, and consult a doctor if needed.

In short, keep the fax stable, dry, well connected, easy to unplug, away from flammable cleaners and metal objects, and avoid putting your hands near moving or pinch-point areas.,['Manual15_4', 'Manual15_5', 'Manual15_6', 'Manual15_7']

To connect the regulator to the LP tank safely, please follow these steps:

1. **Secure the LP tank first**  
   Make sure the LP tank is installed upright and properly secured on the grill. The tank valve should face the front of the cart so the hose is not stretched, twisted, or exposed to damage.

2. **Turn everything OFF**  
   Turn all grill control knobs to the **OFF** position.

3. **Close the LP tank valve**  
   Turn the OPD hand wheel on the LP tank **clockwise until it stops**. This closes the tank valve.

4. **Remove the protective cap**  
   Remove the protective cap from the LP tank valve. Keep the cap and strap attached to the tank so you can reinstall the cap when the tank is disconnected.

<PIC>

Important: **Do not use a POL transport plug** — the plastic plug with external threads. Using it can defeat the safety feature of the LP tank valve.

<PIC>

5. **Align the regulator straight with the tank valve**  
   Hold the regulator firmly and insert the regulator nipple into the LP tank valve. Keep the regulator in a straight line with the valve opening so the threads do not cross-thread. The nipple must be centered in the tank valve.

<PIC>

6. **Hand-tighten the coupling nut**  
   Turn the coupling nut **clockwise** by hand until it reaches a full stop. You may feel resistance as the regulator seals against the tank valve; after that, tighten an additional **1/2 to 3/4 turn** by hand to complete the connection.

   Do **not** use tools, pliers, or a wrench. Hand-tightening only is required.

7. **If it does not connect smoothly**  
   If you cannot complete the connection, disconnect the regulator and repeat the alignment and tightening steps. If it still will not connect, do not use that regulator.

After connecting, perform a leak check before using the grill: open the LP tank valve slowly, apply a 50/50 mild soap-and-water solution to the connection area, and check for growing bubbles. If bubbles appear, close the LP tank valve immediately and do not use the grill until the leak is corrected.,['Manual19_16', 'Manual19_17', 'Manual19_18']

For your safety, please perform the leak test outdoors in a well-ventilated area, away from open flames, sparks, smoking materials, and any gas-fired or electrical appliances. Do not use the grill until this leak test has been completed successfully.

**How to leak test the valves, hose, and regulator:**

1. **Turn all grill control knobs to OFF.** Make sure every burner control knob is fully in the OFF position.

2. **Check the regulator connection.** Confirm that the regulator is tightly connected to the LP tank. The coupling nut should be hand-tightened only—do not use tools.

3. **Open the LP tank valve fully.** Turn the OPD hand wheel counterclockwise to open the tank valve completely.  
   - If you hear a rushing sound, turn the gas off immediately. This indicates a major leak at the connection. Do not continue until the issue is corrected.

4. **Apply a leak-test solution.** Mix a 50/50 solution of mild soap and water. Using a clean brush, apply the soapy solution to the connection points shown below, including the tank valve/regulator connection, hose connections, and valve/manifold areas.

<PIC>

5. **Watch for growing bubbles.** If bubbles appear and continue to grow, there is a gas leak. Immediately close the LP tank valve by turning the hand wheel clockwise.

6. **Retighten and retest.** After closing the tank valve, retighten the connections by hand, then repeat the leak test.  
   - If the leak cannot be stopped, do not try to repair the parts yourself and do not use the grill. Contact Sears for replacement parts at **1-800-4-MY-HOME**.

7. **Close the LP tank valve after testing.** When the test is complete, always close the LP tank valve by turning the hand wheel clockwise.

Important safety reminders: never check for leaks with an open flame, do not smoke during the test, and if you smell gas or cannot stop a leak, move away from the grill and contact your gas supplier or the fire department immediately.,['Manual19_20']

For indirect cooking, the key is to cook the food with circulating heat rather than placing it directly over the flame. This is especially useful for poultry, roasts, ribs, and other large or fatty cuts, because it helps them cook slowly and evenly while reducing flare-ups from dripping fat.

<PIC>

Please pay attention to the following points:

1. Burner setup
- Turn on only the burner(s) needed to create heat, and keep the burner directly under the food OFF.
- For smaller meals, one burner can be used, with the food placed away from the active flame.
- This saves fuel and helps maintain gentler heat.

2. Food placement
- Place the food over the unlit burner area, not directly above the flame.
- If cooking fatty meat, this placement helps prevent grease and juices from dripping directly onto a flame, which reduces flare-ups.

3. Keep the lid closed
- Indirect cooking works best with the lid closed, so heat can circulate around the food like an oven.
- Opening the lid too often will release heat and may increase cooking time.

4. Preheat first, then adjust heat
- After lighting, preheat the grill on HIGH for about 15 minutes.
- Then adjust the burners to the desired setting for cooking, usually LOW to MEDIUM for slow roasting or baking-style cooking.

<PIC>

5. Temperature and weather adjustment
- Cooking times may vary depending on outdoor conditions.
- In cold or windy weather, you may need to increase the burner setting to maintain enough cooking temperature.
- Try to monitor the grill temperature and adjust gradually rather than using very high heat.

6. Check doneness safely
- Large cuts and poultry may brown on the outside before the inside is fully cooked, so use a meat thermometer to confirm doneness.
- Whole poultry should reach 165°F.
- Ground meat should reach 160°F.
- Steaks, roasts, and chops of beef, veal, or lamb should reach at least 145°F.
- Pork should reach 160°F.

7. Safety reminders
- Do not leave the grill unattended while cooking.
- Use long-handled barbecue tools and oven mitts to avoid burns.
- If a grease fire occurs, turn the control knobs and LP tank off. Do not use water on a grease fire.
- Keep the grease tray installed and clean it after the grill has completely cooled.

In short, indirect cooking is best done with the food placed away from the active flame, the lid closed, and the heat kept low and steady for slow, even cooking.,['Manual19_36', 'Manual19_37']

Absolutely — here are the key safety tips to follow when using your LP gas grill:

<PIC>

**1. Place the grill in a safe outdoor location**
- Use the grill **outdoors only** in a well-ventilated area.
- **Never** use it in an enclosed or covered area such as a garage, carport, porch, covered patio, or under any overhead structure.
- Keep the grill at least **3 ft. away from walls or surfaces**.
- Keep at least **10 ft. away from anything that can catch fire** or any ignition source, such as pilot lights, electrical appliances, gasoline, or flammable vapors.
- Do not use the grill on or under balconies.

**2. Keep the grill area clear and supervised**
- Do not allow children to operate the grill or play near it.
- Keep the surrounding area free of dry leaves, paper, towels, gasoline, and other flammable materials.
- Do not block the ventilation holes in the bottom or back of the grill.
- Never leave the grill unattended while it is preheating, cooking, or burning off food residue on high.

**3. Use proper protection while cooking**
- Use long-handled barbecue utensils to keep your hands away from heat and flare-ups.
- Wear oven mitts or heat-resistant gloves to help prevent burns and splatters.
- Never move the grill while it is operating or still hot.
- Do not overload side shelves; keep heavy items off them.

**4. Check the gas connection before use**
- Before opening the LP tank valve, check that the coupling nut is tight.
- Make sure the LP tank is in the correct operating position.
- When the grill is not in use, turn **all control knobs OFF** and close the **LP tank valve**.
- If you need to move or disconnect the LP tank, always close the tank valve first and remove the coupling nut.

<PIC>

**5. Watch for grease or gas-related hazards**
- Empty the grease tray after each use, but only after the grill has completely cooled.
- If grease or hot material drips onto the valve, hose, or regulator, turn off the gas supply immediately. Let the grill cool, find and correct the cause, then clean and inspect the valve, hose, and regulator before using the grill again.
- If the regulator becomes frosty, turn off the grill and LP tank valve immediately. This can indicate a tank problem, and the tank should not be used.
- If the regulator makes a humming or whistling sound during use, that can be normal and does not usually affect safe operation.

**6. Light the grill safely**
- Always open the lid before lighting the burners.
- Do not lean over the grill while lighting.
- Never try to light a burner with the lid closed, as unignited gas can build up inside the grill.
- If the burner does not light, turn the control knobs to **OFF**, close the valve, wait **5 minutes** for gas to clear, then try again.

<PIC>

**7. Be careful with grease fires**
- Do not try to put out a grease fire by closing the lid; grills are ventilated, so this will not safely smother the fire.
- Do not use water on a grease fire, as it can cause injury and spread burning grease.
- If a grease fire occurs, turn the burner knobs OFF and close the LP tank valve if it is safe to do so.
- Clean the grill regularly, especially after cooking fatty foods, to reduce grease buildup.

**8. If you smell gas**
1. Shut off gas to the grill.
2. Extinguish any open flame.
3. Open the lid.
4. If the odor continues, stay away from the grill and immediately contact your gas supplier or fire department.

Following these steps will help reduce the risk of fire, burns, gas buildup, and flare-ups while grilling.,['Manual19_23', 'Manual19_24', 'Manual19_25']

Yes. For your 2005 WaveRunner, “cruising limitations” mainly refer to the conditions and riding behaviors you should avoid so the watercraft can be operated safely and predictably. Please follow these limits whenever you ride:

<PIC>

1. Keep a constant lookout
Always scan ahead, to both sides, and behind you for swimmers, floating objects, other watercraft, and boats. Be especially careful in areas where glare, waves, spray, shoreline structures, or other vessels may block your view.

2. Maintain safe speed and distance
Operate defensively at a speed that gives you enough time to react. Keep a safe distance from people, docks, shorelines, boats, and other watercraft. Do not follow directly behind another boat or watercraft, because sudden speed or direction changes can quickly create a collision risk.

3. Do not spray, splash, or crowd others
Do not ride close to people or other vessels just to spray or splash them. This can be dangerous and may make it difficult for others to predict your movement.

4. Avoid sudden or aggressive maneuvers
Avoid sharp turns, wake jumping, or unpredictable maneuvers, especially near other traffic. These actions can make it hard for others to avoid you and can increase the risk of losing control, being thrown from the watercraft, or colliding with another object.

5. Stay away from shallow water and submerged objects
Avoid areas where the water is shallow or where rocks, stumps, debris, sandbars, or other submerged objects may be present.

<PIC>

Never operate the watercraft in water less than 60 cm / 2 ft deep. In shallow water, the hull or jet intake area may strike submerged objects, which can cause damage and may also lead to injury.

6. Ride within your skill level
This is a high-performance watercraft, so ride within your ability and the current water conditions. Sharp turns, jumping wakes, or jumping waves can increase the risk of back or spinal injury, facial injury, and broken bones. Do not jump wakes or waves.

7. Avoid rough weather and poor visibility
Do not operate in rough water, bad weather, fog, heavy rain, or any condition where visibility is poor. Check the weather forecast and current water conditions before setting out, and be alert for changing weather while riding.

8. Do not ride alone far from help
As a good safety practice, avoid riding without another boat or watercraft nearby. If you travel farther from shore than you could comfortably swim back, ride with another vessel nearby while still keeping a safe operating distance.

<PIC>

9. Do not operate at night
This watercraft is not equipped with the lighting required for night operation. Do not operate it after sunset or before dawn, as other boats may not be able to see you clearly and the risk of collision increases.

In short, cruise only in suitable daylight conditions, in water at least 60 cm / 2 ft deep, at a safe and controllable speed, away from swimmers, boats, shallow areas, and rough or low-visibility conditions.,['Manual20_16', 'Manual20_17', 'Manual20_18']

To operate your jetski safely, please follow these key operation requirements before and during every ride:

1. Wear the required safety gear
- Every rider must wear a Coast Guard-approved personal flotation device (PFD) suitable for personal watercraft use.
- Every rider should wear protective lower-body clothing. A wetsuit bottom is strongly recommended; thick, tightly woven, sturdy, snug-fitting clothing such as denim can provide similar protection. Normal swimwear is not enough protection against forceful water entry if you fall or are near the jet thrust nozzle.
- Eye protection is recommended to reduce wind, water spray, and sun glare. Floating eyewear straps are helpful.
- Footwear and gloves are also recommended for better grip and protection.

<PIC>

2. Consider helmet use based on riding conditions
- Helmets meeting Snell or DOT standards are required for IJSBA-sanctioned races.
- For recreational riding, helmet use depends on your riding environment, riding style, traffic, and water conditions. A helmet may help in some impacts, but it can also create risks if it catches water or limits vision/hearing, so choose carefully if you decide to wear one.

3. Do not operate under the influence
- Never operate the jetski after consuming alcohol or taking drugs. This greatly increases the risk of collision, loss of control, injury, or death.

<PIC>

4. Complete checks before riding
- Always perform preoperation checks before starting out.
- Confirm the craft is in safe operating condition, the controls function properly, and the required safety items are on board.
- Make sure a suitable fire extinguisher is carried on board and kept securely in its container.

<PIC>

5. Follow proper riding position requirements
- The operator and passenger should keep both feet in the footwells whenever the jetski is moving.
- Do not allow a child to ride as a passenger if their feet cannot reach the footwell floor.
- The passenger must sit securely and firmly hold on to the operator.
- Do not apply throttle until the passenger is seated, has both feet in the footwells, and is holding on properly.

<PIC>

6. Respect passenger and load limits
- This model is designed for the operator and one passenger only.
- Never carry more than 2 people at one time.
- Do not exceed the maximum load of 160 kg / 353 lb, including operator, passenger, and any cargo.
- If you are new to the craft, practice riding alone first before carrying a passenger, because handling changes with additional weight.

7. Health and rider suitability
- If you are pregnant or in poor health, consult your doctor before riding.
- Minors should be supervised by adults, and local age/training requirements must be followed. A boating safety course may be required depending on your area.

8. Do not modify the watercraft
- Do not attempt to modify the jetski. Modifications can reduce safety and reliability and may make the craft unsafe or illegal to use.

9. Operate defensively on the water
- Constantly scan for swimmers, boats, objects, shallow areas, and changing water conditions.
- Stay away from swimming areas, as swimmers can be difficult to see.
- Keep a safe distance from boats, other personal watercraft, ski ropes, fishing lines, and obstacles.
- Check behind you before turning.
- Avoid sharp turns, aggressive maneuvers, spraying others, or jumping wakes/waves.
- Follow local waterway rules and the applicable “Rules of the Road.”

10. Be aware of basic operating limitations
- Do not operate in water shallower than 60 cm / 2 ft.
- Do not operate after sunset or before dawn, since this craft is not equipped for night operation.
- Avoid rough water, bad weather, or poor visibility.
- Remember that steering depends on jet thrust: if you completely release the throttle, steering response is greatly reduced, especially at higher speeds.

Following these requirements will help protect you, your passenger, other people on the water, and the jetski itself.,['Manual20_19', 'Manual20_20', 'Manual20_22', 'Manual20_21']

Your 2005 WaveRunner jetski has several important watercraft characteristics that affect how it moves, turns, and responds while riding:

1. It is steered by jet thrust, not by a rudder

Your jetski turns by changing the direction of the jet thrust nozzle. When you turn the handlebars, the jet thrust nozzle changes angle, and the watercraft turns in that direction.

Because of this design, throttle input is very important for steering:

- More throttle = stronger jet thrust and sharper turning.
- Less throttle = weaker jet thrust and wider, more gradual turning.
- Throttle released completely = only minimum thrust, so steering ability drops quickly.

If you are moving faster than trolling speed and release the throttle completely, the jetski will rapidly lose steering response. You may still be able to turn slightly right after releasing the throttle, but once the engine slows down, the watercraft will not respond properly to handlebar input until you apply throttle again or slow to trolling speed.

So, if you need to steer away from an object, do not simply let go of the throttle—you need some throttle to maintain steering control.

2. It does not have a true “neutral” position

This watercraft is water-jet propelled, and the jet pump is directly connected to the engine. That means whenever the engine is running, the jet pump can produce thrust and the watercraft may move.

In practical terms:

- There is no true neutral like on many boats or vehicles.
- Even at idle, some movement may occur.
- Always be aware of your surroundings before starting or idling the engine.

3. Engine shut-off system is important for safety

Before clearing weeds, debris, or anything around the jet intake, stop the engine and remove the clip from the engine shut-off switch. The image below shows the clip and engine shut-off switch location.

<PIC>

4. The jet intake and thrust nozzle are critical operating areas

Water is drawn in through the intake grate, pressurized by the jet pump, and expelled through the jet thrust nozzle to move and steer the watercraft. These areas must be treated carefully because moving jet pump parts can be dangerous when the engine is running.

Keep away from the intake grate while the engine is on. Long hair, loose clothing, straps, or similar items can become caught in moving parts. Also, never insert any object into the jet thrust nozzle while the engine is running.

The image below identifies the intake grate and jet thrust nozzle.

<PIC>

5. Best handling comes with practice

Because steering depends on both handlebar position and throttle, this jetski can feel different from vehicles with conventional steering. Practice turning in a wide, open area without swimmers, boats, docks, or other obstructions until you are comfortable with how throttle affects turning.

In short, your jetski’s key characteristics are: jet-thrust steering, reduced steering when off-throttle, no true neutral, constant awareness around the intake/nozzle, and handling that depends heavily on throttle control.,['Manual20_24', 'Manual20_25']

Your jetski is treated as a power-driven vessel, so when you meet other vessels you should follow the boating “Rules of the Road” for the waterway you are operating on. Always ride defensively, keep a safe distance, scan constantly for traffic, swimmers, objects, and markers, and obey any local speed, no-wake, or restricted-area rules.

The basic idea is that, in most encounter situations, one vessel is the “stand-on” vessel and the other is the “give-way” vessel:

- Stand-on vessel: maintain your course and speed so the other operator can predict your movement. However, you must still take action if a collision becomes possible.
- Give-way vessel: take early, clear, and positive action to avoid the stand-on vessel. Normally, slow down, change course, and pass behind the other vessel rather than crossing in front of it.
- If a collision appears unavoidable, neither vessel has an absolute right-of-way. Both operators must take action to avoid the collision.

The diagram below shows the general right-of-way zones around your watercraft. Vessels in the area where you must give way should be treated as stand-on vessels; vessels in the other area normally give way to you, but you must still stay alert and be ready to avoid danger.

<PIC>

There are three common vessel-encounter situations:

1. Meeting head-on

If you and another power-driven vessel are approaching each other head-on and there is a risk of collision, neither vessel has the right-of-way. Both should alter course early to avoid each other. In normal circumstances, each vessel turns to starboard/right, so the other vessel passes on your port/left side.

<PIC>

2. Crossing paths

When two power-driven vessels are crossing and there is a risk of collision, the vessel that has the other vessel on its starboard/right side must keep out of the way.

- If the other vessel is on your starboard/right side, you are the give-way vessel. Slow down or change course early, and usually pass behind it.
- If the other vessel is on your port/left side, you are normally the stand-on vessel. Maintain course and speed, but continue watching closely and be prepared to avoid a collision if the other operator does not give way.

<PIC>

3. Overtaking

If you are passing another vessel from behind, you are always the give-way vessel. Keep well clear and do not cut close across its bow or stern. Stay out of its way until you are safely past and clear.

If another vessel is overtaking you, maintain your speed and direction as much as safely possible so the other operator can steer around you predictably.

Also keep these special situations in mind:

- Narrow channels and bends: keep to the right when safe and practical. Approach blind bends with extra caution and be prepared to signal with a whistle or horn if needed.
- Fishing vessels: vessels actively fishing with nets, lines, or trawls generally have priority, but they still must not block safe passage in narrow channels. Keep a respectful distance from fishing gear and lines.
- Sailing vessels: sailing vessels are normally given priority over power-driven vessels, with exceptions such as when the sailboat is overtaking, when it must keep clear of fishing vessels, or when it should not obstruct a power vessel limited to a narrow channel.
- Buoys and markers: follow local navigation markers, speed zones, restricted areas, and danger signs. Marker systems and local requirements can vary by area.

Because a jetski has no conventional brakes and needs jet thrust for steering, take action early. Do not wait until you are close to another vessel, and do not suddenly make sharp or unpredictable maneuvers. Slow to a safe speed, keep your distance, check behind you before turning, and follow the local boating rules for the specific waters you are riding in.,['Manual20_26', 'Manual20_27', 'Manual20_28']

Sure — here are the recommended steps to remove and reinstall the seat on your WaveRunner. Before you start, make sure the watercraft is stable, the engine is stopped, and the engine shut-off clip/lanyard is removed so the engine cannot be started accidentally. Work from the rear of the seat where the latch is located. To remove the seat 1. Locate the seat latch at the rear of the seat. The latch is positioned at the back edge of the seat and is lifted upward to release the seat. 2. Pull the seat latch upward. 3. While holding the latch released, lift/pull the seat off the watercraft. If it does not come free immediately, avoid forcing it; check that the latch is fully released, then slide/lift the seat away smoothly. To install the seat 1. Align the front of the seat first. Insert the projection at the front of the seat into the stay on the deck. This front engagement point must be seated correctly before locking the rear. 2. Once the front projection is properly engaged, lower the rear of the seat into position. 3. Push down firmly on the rear of the seat until the latch locks securely. 4. After installation, pull up lightly on the rear of the seat to confirm it is locked in place. Also check that the seat sits evenly and does not shift. Please make sure the seat is fully secured before operating the watercraft, as an unlocked seat can move during riding and affect safety.,['Manual20_35', 'Manual20_36', 'Manual20_37']

To open the hood, push the latch 1 down, and then lift the hood up.

<PIC>

To close the hood, push the hood down to lock it in place.

<PIC>

NOTE: Make sure that the hood is properly secured before operating the watercraft.,['Manual20_38', 'Manual20_39']

Yes—there are two different filler caps on the WaveRunner, and both are removed the same way: by turning the cap counterclockwise. The main difference is their location and what they are used for.

1. Fuel tank filler cap
This cap is located on the front deck area. To remove it, grip the cap firmly and turn it counterclockwise until it comes free. After fueling, reinstall it by turning it clockwise and make sure it is tightened securely before operating the watercraft.

<PIC>

2. Oil tank filler cap
This cap is for the oil tank and is located in a separate recessed area on the craft. To remove it, turn the oil tank filler cap counterclockwise. After adding oil, reinstall it by turning it clockwise and confirm it is fully secured.

<PIC>

Before riding, double-check both caps: they should sit properly in place and feel tight. A loose fuel or oil cap can allow leakage, contamination, or water entry, so it is best to verify them each time after refilling.,['Manual20_40', 'Manual20_41']

There are two engine switches to operate:

Engine stop switch: Push this switch (red button) to stop the engine normally.
<PIC>

Engine shut-off switch: Insert the clip on the end of the engine shut-off cord under the engine shut-off switch (black button). The engine will stop automatically when the clip is removed from the switch, such as if the operator falls off the watercraft.
<PIC>

Always attach the engine shut-off cord to your wrist and the clip to the engine shut-off switch before starting the engine. To prevent accidental starting or unauthorized use, always remove the clip from the shut-off switch when the engine is not running.
<PIC>,['Manual20_43', 'Manual20_44', 'Manual20_45']

There are two different levers on the WaveRunner, and they have different purposes:

1. **Choke lever — used mainly for starting a cold engine**

The choke lever helps the engine start when it is cold by providing a richer fuel-air mixture.

- Move the choke lever **① in direction ⓐ** to turn the choke **ON**.
- Move the choke lever **① in direction ⓑ** to turn the choke **OFF**.

Use the choke only when needed for cold starting. After the engine starts and begins running smoothly, move the choke back to the **OFF** position so the engine can run normally.

<PIC>

2. **Throttle lever — used to control engine speed and acceleration**

The throttle lever controls how much power the engine produces.

- **Squeeze** the throttle lever **①** to increase engine speed and accelerate.
- **Release** the throttle lever to reduce engine speed and return toward idle.

Keep in mind that this watercraft needs jet thrust to steer effectively, so at speeds above trolling speed, fully releasing the throttle will greatly reduce steering response. Apply throttle smoothly when turning or accelerating.

<PIC>

In short: the **choke lever** is for cold starting, while the **throttle lever** is for speed control during operation.,['Manual20_46', 'Manual20_47']

The Quick Shift Trim System (QSTS) selector is the control you use to adjust the running trim of your WaveRunner while riding. In simple terms, it changes how high or low the bow rides on the water by moving the jet thrust nozzle up or down vertically.

<PIC>

As shown above, the QSTS selector is located at the left handlebar grip. It works together with the shift lock lever: you squeeze the lock lever first, rotate the selector to the desired trim position, and then release the lever to lock the selector in place.

There are 5 QSTS positions:
1. Two bow down positions — for lowering the bow.
2. Neutral “N” — suitable for most normal riding conditions.
3. Two bow up positions — for raising the bow.

To change the trim angle safely:
1. Reduce engine speed to 3,000 r/min or less.
2. Squeeze the shift lock lever.
3. Turn the QSTS selector to the desired position.
4. Release the shift lock lever so the selector locks into place.

Important: Do not turn the QSTS selector while riding at full throttle, because this can damage the QSTS mechanism.

<PIC>

The image above shows how the five selector positions correspond to the vertical movement of the jet thrust nozzle. When the nozzle angle changes, the thrust direction changes, which adjusts the bow attitude of the watercraft.

How each trim setting affects riding:

Neutral “N”
- Best choice for most general conditions.
- Gives balanced handling, acceleration, and stability.
- Recommended when you are unsure which trim setting to use.

Bow up positions
- Raises the bow while the watercraft is on plane.
- Puts less of the bow in the water, reducing water resistance.
- Can improve straight-ahead acceleration once on plane and may help increase top speed.
- If the watercraft starts “porpoising” — hopping or bouncing up and down — switch back to neutral or select a bow down position.

Bow down positions
- Lowers the bow and places more of the front hull in the water.
- Can help the craft feel more planted and may improve control in certain conditions.
- Useful if the bow feels too light, if the craft is porpoising, or when you want a more stable trim attitude.
- Because more hull is in the water, water resistance increases, so top speed and straight-line acceleration may be reduced compared with bow up.

<PIC>

This image compares the effect of the trim settings: bow up raises the front of the watercraft, neutral keeps a balanced attitude, and bow down lowers the front into the water.

In short: use neutral for normal riding, bow up when you want better straight-line performance on plane, and bow down when you want a lower, more stable bow attitude or need to reduce porpoising.,['Manual20_51', 'Manual20_52', 'Manual20_53']

The fuel meter and hour meter are both part of the multifunction information center on your WaveRunner, and they help you monitor fuel range and service timing while riding.

**Fuel meter**

The fuel meter shows the approximate amount of fuel remaining in the fuel tank. It uses **8 display segments**; the more segments shown, the more fuel is in the tank. Use it as a guide while riding, because the reading can vary depending on riding conditions, fuel movement in the tank, speed, trim, and water conditions.

<PIC>

A general way to read it is:

- **All 8 segments on**: fuel level is high.
- **Fewer segments on**: fuel level is decreasing.
- **Lowest two segments blinking**: fuel is low, about **13 L / 3.4 US gal / 2.9 Imp gal** remaining.

If the fuel level gets low enough, the lowest two fuel segments, the fuel warning indicator, and the warning light may blink, and the buzzer may sound intermittently. When this happens, refuel as soon as possible. The warning should clear after refueling and restarting the engine.

**Hour meter**

The hour meter records the total engine running time since the watercraft was new. It is mainly used to help track maintenance intervals, because service items are usually based on engine operating hours.

<PIC>

For example, if the display shows **123.4 HOUR**, that means the engine has operated for approximately **123.4 hours** total. This stored time remains even if the battery has been disconnected.

In short: the **fuel meter helps you estimate remaining fuel while riding**, and the **hour meter helps you track engine use for maintenance and service planning**.,['Manual20_58', 'Manual20_59']

Yes—inspecting the fuel filter and fuel tank is an important part of keeping your WaveRunner reliable and safe. Please do the check with the engine off, in a well-ventilated area, away from flames/sparks, and turn the fuel cock knob to “OFF” when the engine is not running.

### 1. Inspecting the fuel filter
Your watercraft uses a one-piece, disposable fuel filter. Locate the filter in the engine compartment as shown below:

<PIC>

Check the fuel filter visually for:
- Water inside the filter
- Dirt, discoloration, or contamination
- Cracks, damage, or loose connections
- Any gasoline odor or signs of leakage around the filter area

Replacement timing:
- After the initial 10 hours or first month of operation
- Then every 200 hours or 24 months
- Any time water is found in the filter

Important: Do not try to replace the fuel filter yourself. If it is installed incorrectly, gasoline can leak, creating a fire or explosion risk. If the filter needs replacement or if you see water/contamination, have a Yamaha dealer replace it.

### 2. Inspecting the fuel tank
Locate the fuel tank in the hull area as shown below:

<PIC>

Check the fuel tank and surrounding area for:
- Gasoline leakage around the tank, fittings, and hoses
- Fuel smell inside the hull or engine compartment
- Water in the fuel tank or fuel system
- Damaged, loose, or deteriorated fuel lines
- Moisture, corrosion, or unusual residue near the tank area

Also make sure the fuel tank filler cap is tightened securely before operating the watercraft, as a loose cap can allow water to enter or fuel vapors to escape.

If water is found in the fuel system, if the tank appears contaminated, or if the fuel tank needs cleaning, do not continue operating the watercraft. Have a Yamaha dealer service it, since fuel system cleaning and repair require proper handling to avoid leakage and fire hazards.,['Manual20_81', 'Manual20_82']

Yes. The adjustable sponsons can be repositioned to tune your WaveRunner for either better straight-line speed or stronger cornering grip. Before you start, keep the watercraft securely supported on land, stop the engine, and remove the engine shut-off clip so it cannot be started accidentally.

1. Locate the adjustable sponson on each side of the rear hull, then remove the three mounting bolts marked ① from both sponsons.

<PIC>

2. Remove both sponsons and reinstall them in the position you want. Make sure the left and right sponsons are set to the same height. Do not run one side higher or lower than the other, as this can affect handling balance.

3. Reinstall and tighten the bolts on both sponsons evenly. Final tightening torque should be:

18 N·m / 1.8 kgf·m / 13 ft·lb

<PIC>

Position effect:

<PIC>

- Up position, shown as position A: reduces water resistance. This helps improve straight-ahead acceleration when the craft is on plane and can also improve top speed.
- Down position, shown as position B: gives the watercraft more “hook” in the water. This improves turning grip and cornering performance.

In short, choose the up position if you want more straight-line performance, and choose the down position if you want sharper turning and more hold in corners. After adjustment, check that all bolts are secure before riding.,['Manual20_83', 'Manual20_84', 'Manual20_70']

If the jet intake or impeller is dirty or clogged with weeds, rope, plastic, sand, or other debris, you may notice cavitation: the engine revs up, but thrust/speed drops. If you keep running it this way, the engine can overheat, so clean it as soon as you suspect a blockage.

<PIC>

Please clean it as follows:

1. Move the watercraft to a safe shallow/beached area, then stop the engine first.  
   Do not try to clear the intake while the engine is running.

2. Remove the clip from the engine shut-off switch.  
   This prevents accidental starting. Keep hands, hair, loose clothing, and PFD straps away from the intake grate and jet thrust nozzle—rotating jet pump parts can cause severe injury.

3. Turn the watercraft onto its port/left side to access the underside.  
   Place a clean cloth, mat, or carpeting under the hull to prevent scratches. Support the bow so the handlebars are not bent or damaged.

<PIC>

4. Remove visible weeds and debris from these areas:  
   - Intake grate / jet intake  
   - Around the drive shaft  
   - Impeller area  
   - Pump housing  
   - Jet thrust nozzle

5. Use safe tools only if needed.  
   You can use your hands with gloves, a plastic scraper, or needle-nose pliers for loose debris. Avoid metal prying tools or anything that could nick the impeller, damage the intake grate, or scratch the pump housing.

6. Rinse lightly if there is mud or sand.  
   A gentle freshwater rinse around the intake and nozzle area is fine. Do not force objects deep into the pump, and do not use high pressure directly into seals or bearings.

7. Recheck before restarting.  
   Make sure the intake grate, impeller area, and nozzle are clear, then return the watercraft upright. Start only in water deep enough for safe operation.

If the debris is tightly wrapped around the drive shaft/impeller, if you see damage to the impeller or intake grate, or if cavitation continues after cleaning, have a Yamaha dealer inspect it before riding again.,['Manual20_85', 'Manual20_86']

Here’s how to start your WaveRunner/jetski in the most common situations. The key differences are where you board from, how deep the water is, and whether you have a passenger—but the basic starting sequence is always the same.

## Basic start-up sequence for all situations

1. **Attach the engine shut-off cord to your wrist** before starting.
2. **Insert the clip into the engine shut-off switch.** If the clip is not installed, the engine will not start. This also stops the engine if you fall off.

<PIC>

3. **Press the green START switch** to start the engine.

<PIC>

4. **Look all around before moving off.** Check for swimmers, boats, docks, floating objects, and anything that could block your path.

<PIC>

Important safety note: this watercraft steers using jet thrust. If you completely release the throttle while trying to avoid something, steering response becomes very limited. Use controlled throttle when turning away from objects.

<PIC>

---

## 1. Starting in shallow water

Use this method when launching from a beach or shallow area.

1. **Do not start the engine in water shallower than 60 cm / 2 ft.** Move the watercraft into deeper water first. Starting in too little water can draw sand, weeds, or debris into the jet intake and damage the pump.

<PIC>

2. Board from the side or rear once the water is deep enough.
3. Attach the shut-off cord to your wrist and insert the clip.
4. Grip the handlebars with both hands.
5. Put both feet in the footwells for balance.
6. Press the START switch, look around, then move off smoothly.

---

## 2. Starting from a dock

Use this method when boarding from a pier, floating dock, or marina platform.

1. Board the watercraft from the side carefully.
2. Attach the shut-off cord to your wrist and insert the clip.
3. Push the watercraft away from the dock before starting off, so the hull and jet thrust are clear of the dock.
4. Grip the handlebars with both hands and place both feet in the footwells.
5. Start the engine and move away slowly while checking traffic around you.

<PIC>

---

## 3. Starting after boarding alone from the water

Use this if you are already in the water and need to reboard by yourself.

1. Swim to the rear of the watercraft. Put both hands on the boarding platform, pull yourself up, and grasp the handgrip.

<PIC>

2. Pull yourself up to a kneeling position, then move forward onto the seat and sit astride.

<PIC>

3. Attach the shut-off cord to your wrist and insert the clip.
4. Hold the handlebars firmly with both hands and place both feet in the footwells.
5. Start the engine only after you are stable, then look in all directions before moving off.

<PIC>

---

## 4. Starting with a passenger

Use this method when carrying one passenger. Make sure the total load, including cargo, does not exceed **160 kg / 353 lb**.

1. The operator boards first and sits astride the seat.
2. The operator attaches the shut-off cord to the wrist and inserts the clip.
3. Have the passenger move to the rear and climb on carefully. Both operator and passenger should help balance the watercraft while boarding.

<PIC>

4. Before starting or applying throttle, confirm the passenger:
   - is seated properly behind the operator,
   - has both feet in the footwells,
   - is securely holding onto the operator.

<PIC>

5. Start the engine, check all directions for boats, swimmers, and obstacles, then accelerate smoothly to planing speed.

<PIC>

Important: Do not apply throttle while the passenger is still in the water or climbing aboard. Keep them away from the jet thrust nozzle area.

---

## Quick summary

- **Normal start:** lanyard on wrist → clip installed → press green START.
- **Shallow water:** only start in at least **60 cm / 2 ft** of water.
- **Dock start:** board first, push away from the dock, then start and move off.
- **Reboarding alone:** climb on from the rear, sit securely, then start.
- **With passenger:** operator boards first; passenger must be seated, feet down, and holding on before throttle is applied.

Before every start, always check your surroundings and keep a safe distance from people, boats, docks, and obstacles.,['Manual20_44', 'Manual20_48', 'Manual20_87', 'Manual20_75', 'Manual20_88', 'Manual20_89', 'Manual20_71', 'Manual20_72', 'Manual20_73', 'Manual20_90', 'Manual20_91', 'Manual20_92']

Here are the basic steps for boarding your jetski with one passenger in deep water:

**Important safety warning:** Do **not** apply throttle while the passenger is still in the water or climbing aboard. Serious internal injuries can occur if someone is close to the jet thrust nozzle and water is forced into body cavities. Only apply throttle after the passenger is fully seated, both feet are in the footwells, and they are holding on securely to the operator.

1. **Operator boards first from the rear.**  
   Swim to the back of the watercraft, pull yourself onto the boarding platform, move onto the seat, and sit astride. Attach the engine shut-off cord to your wrist, then install the clip on the engine shut-off switch.

2. **Passenger moves to the rear of the watercraft.**  
   The passenger should approach from the back, not from the side, to help keep the watercraft balanced and to stay away from the jet thrust area.

3. **Passenger climbs aboard and sits behind the operator.**  
   While the passenger is climbing up, both the operator and passenger should keep the watercraft balanced. The operator should stay centered on the seat and avoid sudden leaning.

<PIC>

4. **Confirm the passenger is properly seated.**  
   Before starting off, make sure the passenger is sitting securely behind you, both feet are on the floor of the footwells, and they are holding firmly onto the operator.

<PIC>

5. **Check your surroundings before moving.**  
   Look carefully in all directions for boats, swimmers, obstacles, ropes, or other hazards. Make sure the area around the watercraft is clear.

6. **Start the engine and accelerate smoothly.**  
   Once both riders are stable and the area is clear, start the engine and accelerate to planing speed in a controlled, steady manner.

<PIC>

A few extra tips: the total load, including operator, passenger, and cargo, should not exceed **160 kg / 353 lb**. If the watercraft feels hard to balance at a standstill, take your time, keep weight centered, and have the passenger help steady the craft while boarding. Both riders should always wear an approved PFD and protective clothing suitable for personal watercraft use.,['Manual20_90', 'Manual20_91', 'Manual20_92']

If the jetski feels hard to balance while stopped, use a controlled two-person boarding method and get the craft moving gently as soon as the operator is stable. This is especially important when the total rider/cargo weight is high, but never exceed the maximum load of 160 kg / 353 lb including cargo.

1. Have the passenger stabilize the jetski first. The passenger should stay in the water and hold the craft steady while the operator boards from the rear platform. The operator should pull onto the platform, move into a seated position, and then take over balancing the jetski.
<PIC>

2. Before moving off, the operator should attach the engine shut-off lanyard to the left wrist and install the clip on the engine shut-off switch. Start the engine and keep it at idle. Check all around for swimmers, boats, docks, or other obstacles before applying any throttle.

3. Once the operator is seated and stable, the passenger can climb onto the rear platform. The passenger should first pull up, kneel on the platform, and then carefully crawl forward onto the seat while the craft begins to accelerate smoothly.
<PIC>
