Important: Do not use the pressure cooker without the anti-block shield properly installed, as it is required for safe pressure cooking.,['Manual30_19', 'Manual30_35']

To set the float valve and silicone cap on the pressure cooking lid, please install them on the pressure cooking lid only—not the air fryer lid.

1. Insert the float valve from the top of the lid. Hold the pressure cooking lid with the top side facing up, then drop the narrow end of the float valve into the float valve hole. The flat, wider top of the float valve should remain visible on the top of the lid.

<PIC>

2. Keep the float valve in place while turning the lid over. Place one finger on the flat top of the float valve so it does not fall out, then carefully flip the lid over to access the underside.

3. Attach the silicone cap underneath. Push the silicone cap firmly onto the bottom end of the float valve until it is fully seated. It should fit snugly and hold the float valve in place.

<PIC>

4. Check that it is installed correctly. From the top of the lid, the float valve should be able to move up and down freely. From the underside, the silicone cap should be securely attached and not loose or tilted.

Please do not use the pressure cooker if the float valve or silicone cap is missing, loose, damaged, or not properly fitted. These two parts work together to seal steam during pressure cooking. Also, during or after cooking, do not try to open the lid while the float valve is raised; wait until it drops back down before opening.,['Manual30_36', 'Manual30_37']

To install the condensation collector, place it on the back of the cooker base. This small cup catches liquid that may run off from the condensation rim during cooking, so it should be attached before you start cooking.

Steps:

1. Make sure the cooker base is cool, unplugged, and sitting on a stable, level surface.
2. Go to the back of the cooker base and locate the small mounting tabs.
3. Hold the condensation collector with its grooves facing the tabs on the cooker base.
4. Align the grooves on the collector over the tabs.
5. Slide the condensation collector straight into place until it sits securely against the back of the cooker base.

<PIC>

After each use, remove the collector by pulling it straight away from the cooker base — do not pull it downward. Empty any collected liquid, rinse it, dry it, and reinstall it before the next cooking session.,['Manual30_38']

To install the sealing ring on your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer, please use the pressure cooking lid and follow these steps:

1. Turn the pressure cooking lid over so you can see the underside of the lid.
2. Locate the circular stainless-steel sealing ring rack around the inside of the lid.
3. Place the silicone sealing ring over the sealing ring rack.
4. Working around the lid a section at a time, press the sealing ring firmly into place behind the rack.
5. Continue pressing all the way around until the ring sits evenly with no twists, gaps, or puckering.

<PIC>

A correctly installed sealing ring should sit snugly behind the sealing ring rack. After installation, turn the lid over gently—the sealing ring should stay in place and should not fall out.

Before cooking, also check that:
- Only one sealing ring is installed at a time.
- The ring is not stretched, cracked, cut, deformed, or damaged.
- The ring is seated evenly all the way around the lid.
- You are using an authorized sealing ring made for this multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer.

If the sealing ring looks loose, puckered, or does not stay in place when the lid is turned over, remove it and reinstall it before using pressure cooking functions.,['Manual30_31']

To remove the silicone sealing ring from the pressure cooking lid, please follow these steps:

1. Make sure the cooker is unplugged, fully cooled, and not pressurized before handling the lid. The float valve should be down before the lid is removed.

2. Turn the pressure cooking lid over so you can see the underside of the lid and the silicone sealing ring.

3. Grip the edge of the silicone sealing ring with your fingers, then gently pull it outward and away from behind the circular stainless-steel sealing ring rack. Work your way around the lid if needed until the entire ring comes free.

<PIC>

4. After removing the ring, check the stainless-steel sealing ring rack. It should be centered, securely attached, and even in height all the way around the lid. If the rack looks bent, loose, or deformed, do not try to reshape or repair it.

5. If the sealing ring is difficult to remove, a little warm soapy water can help—the silicone is usually easier to remove and reinstall when it is wet and soapy.

Before using the cooker again, inspect the sealing ring for cuts, stretching, deformation, or damage. If it is damaged or no longer fits snugly, replace it with an authorized sealing ring for your model.,['Manual30_30']

The sealing ring is a removable silicone ring fitted on the underside of the pressure cooking lid, around the circular stainless-steel sealing ring rack. It is used for pressure-cooking functions, where it helps create an airtight seal between the lid and the stainless-steel inner pot.

<PIC>

When the pressure cooking lid is closed correctly, the sealing ring seals the gap between the lid and inner pot so steam can build pressure inside the cooker. It must be installed before using pressure-based cooking programs, and only one sealing ring should be installed in the lid at a time.

<PIC>

For air fryer use, the sealing ring is not what creates the cooking environment. Air frying, roasting, baking, grilling, and dehydrating use the air fryer lid and hot-air circulation rather than pressure. However, the sealing ring still belongs to the pressure cooking lid, so you should keep it clean, dry, and properly seated when storing or switching back to pressure cooking.

A few important care and safety points:

- Use only authorized sealing rings made for this multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer.
- Do not use a sealing ring that is stretched, cracked, cut, deformed, loose, or damaged.
- Check the sealing ring before pressure cooking to make sure it is clean, correctly installed, and sitting evenly all the way around the rack.
- Silicone can absorb strong smells and flavors, so it is a good idea to keep separate rings for savory dishes, sweet dishes, or strongly aromatic foods.
- With normal use, the sealing ring should be replaced every 12–18 months, or sooner if you notice stretching, deformation, odor retention, or any visible damage.

To remove the sealing ring, grip the edge of the silicone and pull it out from behind the circular stainless-steel sealing ring rack. After removing it, check that the rack is secure, centered, and even all the way around.

<PIC>

To install the sealing ring, place it over the sealing ring rack and press it firmly into place all the way around. Make sure there are no puckers, twists, or sections sitting outside the rack. When installed correctly, the ring should fit snugly behind the rack and should not fall out when the lid is turned over.

<PIC>

If steam escapes from the sides of the lid during pressure cooking, press Cancel, allow the cooker to depressurize safely, and then check the sealing ring. The most common causes are that the ring is missing, not seated evenly, dirty, stretched, or damaged. Also confirm that the lid is fully closed and the inner pot rim is clean and not misshapen.,['Manual30_4', 'Manual30_16', 'Manual30_30', 'Manual30_31']

The steam release valve is a key pressure-control and safety part used with the pressure cooking lid of your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer. It is not part of the air fryer lid; it is used when pressure cooking or steaming under pressure.

You can find it on the top of the pressure cooking lid, near the float valve and the quick release button. The float valve shows whether the cooker is still pressurized, while the quick release button operates the steam release valve.

<PIC>

Here is how the parts work together:

- Steam release valve: the outlet where steam is released.
- Quick release button: controls whether the steam release valve is sealing or venting.
- Float valve: indicates pressure status. If it is raised, the cooker is still pressurized and the lid must not be opened. When it drops down flush with the lid, pressure has been released.

The quick release button has two positions:

1. Seal position — keeps pressure inside the cooker during pressure cooking.
2. Vent position — opens the steam release path so pressure can be released.

<PIC>

During Quick Release, press the quick release button down until it clicks into the Vent position. Steam will eject upward from the top of the steam release valve. This is normal, but the steam is extremely hot, so keep your hands, face, cabinets, and any exposed skin away from the valve area.

<PIC>

Important safety notes:

- Do not cover, block, or place anything over the steam release valve.
- Do not lean over the valve when releasing pressure.
- Do not try to open the lid while the float valve is raised.
- If cooking thick, oily, starchy, or expanding foods such as stews, pasta, congee, beans, or grains, Natural Release is usually safer than Quick Release because rapid venting may cause splattering or foaming.
- Before pressure cooking, check that the steam release valve, steam release pipe, float valve, and anti-block shield are clean and not clogged.

For cleaning or checking the valve, pull the steam release valve straight up and off the steam release pipe. It is designed to come off for cleaning, but it must be reinstalled before pressure cooking.

<PIC>

To reinstall it, place the steam release valve back onto the steam release pipe and press down firmly. When installed correctly, it will sit loosely on the pipe; that slight looseness is normal. However, it should still stay in place when the lid is turned over.

<PIC>

In short, the steam release valve is the cooker’s controlled steam outlet. The quick release button tells it whether to seal or vent, and the float valve confirms whether it is safe to open the lid.,['Manual30_3', 'Manual30_14', 'Manual30_15', 'Manual30_32', 'Manual30_33']

The quick release button is the pressure-release control on the pressure cooking lid. It works with the steam release valve to decide whether the cooker keeps pressure inside or vents steam out.

<PIC>

It has two main positions:

- Seal position: the button is popped up. Use this when pressure cooking, because the cooker needs to build and maintain pressure.
- Vent position: the button is pressed down until it clicks and locks. This opens the steam release path and lets pressure escape quickly.

To do a Quick Release, press the quick release button down until it clicks into the Vent position.

<PIC>

Once it is in Vent, hot steam will shoot upward from the steam release valve. This is normal. Keep your hands, face, and exposed skin away from the steam path. When all pressure has been released, the float valve will drop down into the lid; only then is it safe to open the pressure cooking lid.

<PIC>

A few important things to know:

- The button is designed to keep your hand farther from the steam compared with manually turning a valve.
- Although the lid marking may say “Turn to Seal,” the motion is more like a light flick back to Seal. Do not rotate it more than about 45°, and let it spring back to its normal position.
- Do not cover or block the steam release valve.
- Do not force the lid open while the float valve is still raised; that means the cooker is still pressurized.
- Quick Release is best for foods that can overcook easily, such as quick-cooking vegetables or delicate seafood.
- Avoid Quick Release for fatty, oily, thick, foamy, high-starch, or expanding foods, such as stews, chili, pasta, congee, oatmeal, beans, and grains. These are more likely to splatter or clog the steam path, so Natural Release is usually safer for them.

For air frying functions, the quick release button is generally not involved, because air frying does not build pressure. It is mainly used when the pressure cooking lid is on and the appliance has been pressure cooking.,['Manual30_14', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_01', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_02']

Natural Release, also called NR or NPR, is a pressure-release method used after pressure cooking. Instead of manually venting steam right away, you simply let the cooker cool down on its own so the internal pressure drops gradually.

<PIC>

Here’s what it means in practice:

1. After the pressure cooking program ends, leave the quick release button in the popped-up Seal position. Do not press it down to Vent.
2. Turn Keep Warm off if you want the pressure to reduce more naturally and avoid extra heat being applied.
3. Wait until the pressure releases by itself. The food will continue to cook slightly during this time because the pot is still hot and pressurized.
4. When the float valve drops down into the lid, the cooker is fully depressurized and it is safe to open the pressure cooking lid.

Natural Release can take different amounts of time depending on the amount of food, liquid, and ingredients inside the pot. In some cases, it may take up to about 40 minutes.

NR/NPR is especially recommended for foods that may foam, splatter, or expand, such as soups, stews, chili, pasta, oatmeal, congee, beans, and grains. Using Natural Release with these foods helps prevent hot liquid or starchy foam from spraying out of the steam release valve.

The main difference from Quick Release is that Quick Release stops cooking faster by venting steam immediately, while Natural Release is slower and gentler. Quick Release is better for delicate foods like vegetables or seafood, but Natural Release is safer and more suitable for thick, starchy, or expanding foods.

Please note that Natural Release only applies to pressure cooking functions. It is not used for air frying modes such as Air Fry, Roast, Bake, Grill, or Dehydrate, because those functions do not build pressure.,['Manual30_9']

The robot anatomy of the vacuum is organized into three main areas: the top view, the control buttons and status indicators, and the bottom view. Here’s what each part is and what it does.

**TOP VIEW**
<PIC>

- **iAdapt® Localization Camera**: Helps the robot recognize its position and navigate around your home more efficiently.
- **Handle**: Allows you to safely lift and carry the robot when needed.
- **Bin Release Button**: Press this button to remove the dust bin for emptying or cleaning.

**BUTTONS & INDICATORS**
<PIC>

**Buttons:**
- **DOCK Button**: Sends the robot back to its charging base.
- **CLEAN Button**: Wakes the robot, starts or pauses a cleaning cycle, and can also be used for certain reset/reboot actions.
- **SPOT Button**: Starts spot cleaning, where the robot focuses on a small, localized area of debris.

**Indicators:**
- **Wi‑Fi® Indicator**: Shows Wi‑Fi connection status.
- **Troubleshooting Indicator**: Lights up when the robot detects an issue that may need attention.
- **Battery Indicator**: Shows charging or battery status.
- **Full Bin Indicator**: Lets you know when the dust bin needs to be emptied.
- **Dirt Detect™ Indicator**: Lights when the robot detects a dirtier area and is cleaning it more thoroughly.

**BOTTOM VIEW**
<PIC>

- **Debris Extractors**: The main cleaning rollers that lift dirt, hair, and debris from the floor.
- **Extractor Caps**: End caps on the extractors that can be removed to clear hair or debris buildup.
- **Extractor Frame**: Holds the debris extractors in place during cleaning.
- **Extractor Frame Release Tabs**: Used to open the extractor frame when removing or cleaning the extractors.
- **Dust Bin**: Stores the dirt and debris collected during cleaning.
- **Side Brush**: Sweeps dust and debris from edges and corners into the cleaning path.
- **Charging Contacts**: Metal contact points that connect with the charging base to recharge the robot.
- **Floor Tracking Sensor**: Helps the robot track floor movement and navigate accurately while cleaning.,['Manual32_1', 'Manual32_2', 'Manual32_3']

The two primary modes are Halo Mode and Virtual Wall Mode. You can set the device to one of two modes to fit your home's cleaning needs.

<PIC>

Halo Mode: When the switch is in the "down" position, the device creates a protective zone that vacuum will not enter. This prevents vacuum from bumping into items you want to protect, such as a dog bowl or vase, or crossing into undesired areas.

<PIC>

Virtual Wall Mode: When the switch is in the "up" position, the device functions as a virtual wall. It can block openings of up to 10 feet by creating an invisible, cone-shaped barrier that only vacuum can see.

<PIC>,['Manual32_4', 'Manual32_5', 'Manual32_6']

To empty the vacuum cleaner’s bin, please follow these steps:

1. **Pause the vacuum if it is currently cleaning.**  
   If the Full Bin indicator turns on during a cleaning job, pause the cleaning cycle first so you can safely remove and empty the bin.

2. **Remove the dust bin.**  
   Locate the **bin release button** on the vacuum, press it, and pull the bin out from the vacuum body.

<PIC>

3. **Empty the bin into a trash container.**  
   Hold the bin over a trash can, open the bin door, and let the debris fall out. You can gently tap the bin to remove any dust or debris stuck inside.

<PIC>

4. **Check the filter area before reinstalling.**  
   After emptying the bin, it’s a good habit to check the filter and remove loose dust if needed. Keeping the filter clean helps maintain suction performance.

5. **Close the bin door and reinstall the bin.**  
   Make sure the bin door is fully closed, then slide the bin back into the vacuum until it seats securely. The vacuum should be ready to continue or start cleaning again.

**Tip:** Empty the bin after each use. If the Full Bin indicator stays on even after the bin is empty, the bin sensors may need to be wiped with a clean, dry cloth.,['Manual32_8', 'Manual32_9']

Here is the correct way to clean the vacuum cleaner filter:

1. **Remove and empty the dust bin first.** Press the bin release button, take out the dust bin, and empty the debris into a trash container. This prevents loose dirt from falling back into the vacuum while you clean the filter.

2. **Take out the filter by the yellow tab.** Open the filter compartment and gently pull the filter out using the yellow tab—avoid pulling on the pleated filter material itself.

<PIC>

3. **Clean the filter by tapping it over a trash container.** Hold the filter by the tab and tap it against the inside edge of the trash container to shake off dust and debris. Do not use water or cleaning liquid; keep the filter dry.

<PIC>

4. **Reinstall the filter correctly.** Put the filter back into the compartment with the **yellow tab facing up**. Make sure it is fully seated, because the filter door will not close properly unless the filter is inserted correctly.

5. **Close the bin and reinstall it into the vacuum.** Confirm the dust bin clicks back into place before starting another cleaning cycle.

For best performance, clean the filter **once per week**, or **twice per week if you have pets**. The filter should generally be replaced about **every 2 months** to maintain strong suction and filtration.,['Manual32_10', 'Manual32_11']

To clean the full bin sensors properly, please follow these steps. We recommend doing this about once every 2 weeks, or whenever the Full Bin indicator stays on even though the bin is not full.

1. Pause/stop the vacuum and remove the dust bin  
Make sure the vacuum is not actively cleaning. Press the bin release button, pull out the dust bin, and empty all debris from it before cleaning the sensors.
<PIC>

2. Clean the sensors on the vacuum body  
With the bin removed, locate the two full bin sensors inside the bin compartment. Gently wipe both sensors with a clean, dry cloth to remove dust or buildup. Do not use water, wet wipes, sprays, or cleaning solution on the sensors.
<PIC>

3. Clean the sensor ports on the dust bin  
Next, wipe the sensor ports on the bin itself. Be sure to clean both areas:
- The inner sensor ports inside the bin door
- The outer sensor ports on the outside of the bin

Use only a clean, dry cloth, and make sure no dust is blocking the sensor openings.
<PIC>

4. Reinstall the bin and check the indicator  
After the sensors and ports are clean and dry, place the bin back into the vacuum until it is fully seated. Then restart cleaning or check whether the Full Bin indicator has cleared.

If the Full Bin indicator still remains on after cleaning, remove and reinsert the bin once more to confirm it is properly installed, then reboot the vacuum if needed.,['Manual32_12', 'Manual32_13', 'Manual32_14']

To clean the vacuum’s extractors/rollers, please follow these steps:

1. **Prepare the vacuum safely**
   - Pause or stop the cleaning cycle, then turn the vacuum over on a flat, stable surface.
   - Make sure the vacuum is not on the charging dock while you are cleaning or maintaining it.
   - Remove loose hair, string, or debris by hand; avoid spraying liquid directly into the vacuum.

2. **Open the extractor frame**
   Pinch the **yellow extractor frame release tabs**, then lift the extractor frame upward. Remove any visible obstructions from the frame area.
   <PIC>

3. **Remove the extractors and clean the capped ends**
   Take out both extractors/rollers. Remove the **yellow extractor caps** from the ends, then clear away any hair, lint, or debris wrapped around the caps and the metal axles. After cleaning, reinstall the caps securely.
   <PIC>

4. **Clean the opposite ends of the extractors**
   Check the other side of each extractor, especially around the **square and hexagonal plastic pegs**. Pull out any hair or debris that may be packed into or wrapped around these areas.
   <PIC>

5. **Clear the vacuum path**
   With the extractors removed, inspect the open channel where the rollers sit. Remove any debris blocking the suction path so airflow is not restricted.
   <PIC>

6. **Reinstall the extractors correctly**
   Place the extractors back into the cleaning head module. Match each extractor’s **peg color and shape** with the matching color and shape icons on the module. Once installed, lower the extractor frame until it sits securely in place.

7. **Final check**
   Rotate the extractors gently by hand to confirm they are seated properly and not jammed. If the vacuum has been picking up less debris than usual, cleaning the extractors along with emptying the bin and cleaning the filter can help restore performance.

For best performance, clean the extractors about **once every 4 months**, or **once every 3 months in homes with pets**. Replace them if they are worn, damaged, or no longer cleaning effectively.,['Manual32_19', 'Manual32_20', 'Manual32_21', 'Manual32_22']

To clean the vacuum’s sensors and charging contacts properly, please follow these steps:

1. **Turn the vacuum off / pause cleaning and remove it from the Home Base.** Make sure your hands are dry before handling the vacuum or the charging dock.

2. **Clean the sensors with a clean, dry cloth.** Gently wipe each sensor area to remove dust, fingerprints, or debris. Do not spray cleaning solution directly onto the sensors or into any sensor openings, as moisture can affect sensor performance.

<PIC>

3. **Clean the charging contacts on the vacuum.** Turn the vacuum over and locate the two metal charging contacts on the underside. Wipe them with a clean, dry cloth until they are free of dust or buildup.

4. **Clean the charging contacts on the Home Base.** Wipe the matching metal contacts on the charging dock with the same type of clean, dry cloth. This helps ensure the vacuum can dock and charge reliably.

<PIC>

5. **Check before using again.** Confirm the sensor areas and charging contacts are dry and unobstructed, then place the vacuum back on the Home Base to verify that it charges normally.

For best performance, clean the sensors and charging contacts about **once per month**, or more often if you notice docking, navigation, or charging issues.,['Manual32_17', 'Manual32_18']

To position the Home Base correctly, place it in a clear, open area where the vacuum can easily leave, return, and dock without obstacles.

Recommended placement:
- Leave at least 1.5 ft / 0.5 m of clear space on each side of the Home Base.
- Leave at least 4 ft / 1.2 m of clear space in front of it.
- Keep it at least 4 ft / 1.2 m away from stairs or drop-offs.
- Keep it at least 8 ft / 2.4 m away from virtual wall barriers.

<PIC>

Additional tips for best docking performance:
- Keep the Home Base plugged in at all times so the vacuum can recharge automatically.
- Place it flat against a wall on a level floor, not at an angle.
- Avoid placing it under furniture, behind doors, in tight corners, or near loose cords.
- Make sure the area has stable Wi-Fi coverage if you use the app, so the vacuum can receive updates and status information.
- Keep the charging contacts on both the vacuum and the Home Base clean and dry to help ensure reliable charging.,['Manual32_18']

To clean the front caster wheel, please follow these steps:

1. **Prepare the vacuum safely**  
   Pause/turn off the vacuum and remove it from the charging dock. Turn the vacuum upside down on a soft, stable surface so you can access the front caster wheel.

2. **Remove the front caster wheel**  
   Hold the front wheel assembly and pull it straight upward with firm, steady pressure. Avoid twisting too hard, as this may damage the housing.

   <PIC>

3. **Clean the wheel cavity**  
   Remove dust, hair, string, or other debris from inside the wheel cavity. A dry cloth, small brush, or tweezers can help clear buildup. Do not pour or spray liquid into the opening.

4. **Check wheel rotation**  
   Spin the wheel by hand. If it turns freely, you can reinstall it. If it feels stiff, noisy, or blocked, remove the wheel from its housing and push firmly to slide out the axle.

5. **Clear the axle and wheel**  
   Remove any hair, thread, or debris wrapped around the axle or trapped inside the wheel. Make sure the axle area is clean before reassembly.

6. **Reinstall the parts**  
   Place the axle and wheel back into the housing, then press the front caster wheel assembly back into the vacuum until it clicks securely into place.

7. **Final check**  
   Spin the wheel again to confirm it rotates smoothly and sits firmly in the housing. For best performance, clean the front caster wheel about once a month, or more often if you have pets or long hair on the floor.,['Manual32_15']

To clean the side brush properly, please follow these steps:

1. **Turn the vacuum off and place it upside down** on a flat, stable surface. Make sure it is not running before maintenance.

2. **Remove the side brush screw.** Use a coin or a small screwdriver to loosen and remove the screw located in the center of the side brush.

<PIC>

3. **Take off the side brush.** Lift the brush away from the brush post.

4. **Clean the brush and brush post thoroughly.** Remove any tangled hair, threads, dust, or debris from the brush bristles and around the mounting post. If needed, gently pull off wrapped hair by hand or use a small cleaning tool.

5. **Reinstall the side brush.** Place the brush back onto the brush post, align it correctly, then reinstall and tighten the screw. Do not overtighten—just make sure the brush is securely fixed.

6. **Check rotation before use.** Spin the side brush lightly by hand to confirm it moves freely and is not blocked.

For best performance, clean the side brush about once a month, or more often if you have pets or notice hair wrapping around it frequently.,['Manual32_16']

The dual mode virtual wall barrier keeps the vacuum in the places you want to be cleaned and out of the ones you don’t. You can set it to one of two modes to fit your home’s cleaning needs.

<PIC>

Halo Mode: When the switch is in the “down” position, the device creates a protective zone that the vacuum will not enter. This helps prevent the vacuum from bumping into items you want to protect, such as a dog bowl or vase, or crossing into undesired areas. The halo reaches approximately 24 inches from the center of the device.

<PIC>

Virtual Wall Mode: When the switch is in the “up” position, the device functions as a virtual wall. It can block openings of up to 10 feet by creating an invisible, cone-shaped barrier that only the vacuum can see.

<PIC>,['Manual32_4', 'Manual32_5', 'Manual32_6']

If the vacuum indicates a problem, first identify the alert, then check the most common causes before rebooting.

When there is an issue, the vacuum will usually make a two-tone distress sound, play a spoken error message, and the troubleshooting indicator will blink. The troubleshooting indicator is the warning-triangle icon on the control panel.

<PIC>

Please try the following steps:

1. Listen to the spoken message
   - The spoken message is the best clue to what needs attention.
   - If available, also check the app for the current vacuum status and any related guidance.

2. Pause the cleaning cycle safely
   - Press CLEAN once to pause the vacuum.
   - Move it away from stairs, cords, loose clothing, toys, or other obstacles before inspecting it.

3. Check the bin and full-bin indicator
   - If the bin is full, remove and empty it, then reinstall it securely.
   - If the full-bin indicator is on but the bin does not look full, wipe the full-bin sensors and the sensor ports on the bin with a clean, dry cloth.

4. Check for anything blocking movement or suction
   - Turn the vacuum over and inspect the extractors, side brush, front caster wheel, and vacuum path.
   - Remove hair, string, dust buildup, or debris that may be wrapped around the rollers, brush, wheel, or axles.
   - Make sure all parts are reinstalled firmly before restarting.

5. Clean the sensors and charging contacts
   - Wipe the floor sensors, floor tracking sensor, and charging contacts with a clean, dry cloth.
   - Do not spray liquid directly onto sensors, sensor openings, or charging contacts.

6. Check the charging area if the problem is power-related
   - Make sure the charging contacts on both the vacuum and the dock are clean and dry.
   - Confirm the dock is plugged in and placed in an open, uncluttered area.

7. Reboot the vacuum if the issue continues
   - Press and hold CLEAN for 10 seconds, until all indicators illuminate, then release.
   - After releasing CLEAN, you should hear a tone confirming the reboot was successful.
   - If you use scheduled cleaning, open the app afterward to confirm the schedule is still set correctly.

8. Test again
   - Press CLEAN to restart a cleaning cycle.
   - If the same alert returns after the bin, sensors, brushes, wheels, extractors, and charging contacts have been checked, note the spoken message and contact support with that exact message for further assistance.,['Manual32_2']

To mount the camera or compatible equipment to a drop-ceiling T-rail, use the included T-rail clips, screws, rubber spacers if needed, and hex key. The mounting hardware is designed to fit most 9/16", 15/16", and 1 1/2" T-rails.

1. Confirm how the T-rail clips attach to the rail.
Position each clip under the T-rail flange, then rotate the clip until it snaps onto the rail. The clip should sit flat and grip the rail securely.
<PIC>

2. Set the correct clip spacing using the mount plate template.
Use the dashed guide lines on the template to align the two T-rail clips. The clips must line up with the mount plate holes marked “G”, which are the holes used for drop-ceiling T-rail mounting. This spacing is important so the mount plate can be fastened evenly without forcing the screws.
<PIC>

3. Tighten the clip set screws.
Once the clips are in the correct position, tighten the set screws on the T-rail clips using the included 5/64" / 2 mm hex key. Tighten them enough to hold the clips firmly in place, but do not overtighten to the point of deforming the rail or clip.
<PIC>

4. Attach the mount plate to the T-rail clips.
Hold the mount plate against the clips and fasten it through the holes marked “G”. Make sure the plate is seated evenly and the screws thread cleanly into the clips.
<PIC>

5. Choose the correct screw setup for your ceiling type.
For a standard T-rail installation, use the standard 6-32 x 4 mm T-rail screws.

For a recessed T-rail installation, use the rubber spacers together with the longer 6-32 x 7 mm screws. The spacers fill the gap created by the recessed rail so the mount plate sits securely and does not flex.
<PIC>

Final checks before attaching the camera or equipment:
- The T-rail clips should be fully snapped onto the rail.
- The black foam pads should be slightly compressed after installation.
- The mount plate should not wobble or shift when gently pulled.
- The screws should be snug and properly aligned with the “G” holes.
- If the rail is recessed, confirm the rubber spacers are installed between the mount plate and clips before tightening.

After the mount plate is secure, route the Ethernet/PoE cable as needed, attach the camera or equipment to the mount plate, and secure it with the appropriate safety screw if provided.,['Manual33_10', 'Manual33_11', 'Manual33_12', 'Manual33_13', 'Manual33_14']

To power the camera, use Power over Ethernet (PoE):

1. Remove the cable guard on the camera so the Ethernet cable can be routed properly.
2. Connect one end of an Ethernet cable to the camera’s Ethernet/PoE port.
3. Route the cable through the camera body or cable channel as needed, making sure it is not sharply bent or pinched.

<PIC>

4. Connect the other end of the Ethernet cable to an active 802.3af PoE switch port or to a PoE injector.
5. After connection, check the front LED to confirm the camera is receiving power and starting up:
   - Flashing green: the camera is initializing or updating.
   - Solid green: the camera is operating normally.

Note: PoE Ethernet cable length should not exceed 300 ft / 100 m.,['Manual33_3']

The brake lever and parking brake button are used for two different purposes:

1. **Using the brake lever to slow down or stop**

To slow down or stop the machine, first **release the throttle**, then **squeeze the brake lever smoothly toward the handlebar**. The brake works by slowing the drive system, so apply it gradually rather than suddenly, especially on ice or hard-packed snow where stopping distance is longer.

<PIC>

When braking, avoid grabbing the lever abruptly. Sudden or excessive braking can cause the drive track to lose traction, which may reduce control of the machine.

2. **Brake lever position check**

Make sure the end of the brake lever does **not extend beyond the end of the handlebar**. This helps prevent the brake lever from being damaged if the machine is placed on its side for service or handling.

<PIC>

3. **Using the parking brake button**

Use the parking brake when **parking the machine** or **before starting the engine**.

To apply the parking brake:
- Squeeze the brake lever toward the handlebar.
- While holding the lever, push down the parking brake button.
- Release the brake lever so the brake stays locked in place.

<PIC>

To release the parking brake, simply **squeeze the brake lever again**. The parking brake button will disengage and the lever will return to normal operation.

Important safety notes:
- Always set the parking brake before starting the engine.
- Do not ride or run the machine with the parking brake applied, as this can overheat the brake disc and reduce braking performance.
- Before moving off, confirm the parking brake has been fully released and test the brake at low speed to make sure it is working properly.,['Manual34_39', 'Manual34_40', 'Manual34_133']

Before riding a snowmobile, take a few minutes to do a full pre-operation check. These checks help prevent loss of control, brake failure, fuel/oil problems, and damage to the track or drive system.

1. Confirm you understand the controls and safety labels
Read all safety labels on the snowmobile and make sure you know the function of the throttle, brake, steering, engine stop switch, parking brake, fuel cap, and any drive/reverse selector before starting.
<PIC>

2. Check throttle, steering, and brake operation before starting
Operate the throttle lever and confirm it moves smoothly and returns fully to the idle position when released. Turn the handlebars left and right to confirm smooth steering movement. Test the brake lever for firm, reliable response. If any control sticks, feels loose, or does not return normally, do not ride until it is corrected.
<PIC>

3. Set the parking brake before starting, then release it before riding
Apply the parking brake before attempting to start the engine. After the engine is running and you are ready to move, make sure the parking brake is fully released. Riding with the parking brake applied can overheat the brake disc and reduce braking ability.

4. Check fuel level and refueling safety
Make sure there is enough fuel for the trip, and confirm the fuel tank cap is securely closed after refueling. Refuel outdoors, keep snow/ice out of the tank, never refuel near flames or while smoking, and wipe up spilled fuel immediately.
<PIC>

5. Check engine oil level
Make sure there is sufficient oil in the oil tank before operating. Low oil can lead to serious engine damage, so top up with the recommended 2-cycle oil when needed and secure the oil cap properly.
<PIC>

6. Inspect the starter rope
If the snowmobile uses a manual/recoil starter, check that the starter rope is not frayed, damaged, or close to breaking. A damaged rope can fail when starting and leave you stranded.
<PIC>

7. Inspect key drive and chassis components
Before every ride, look over the drive V-belt and V-belt guard, drive track, slide runners, skis and ski runners, steering linkage, lights, battery condition, and all visible fittings/fasteners. Do not operate the snowmobile if the drive belt/guard is missing, the track is damaged or misadjusted, the steering has excessive play, or critical fasteners are loose.

8. Wear proper protective clothing
Wear an approved helmet, eye protection or face shield, warm snowmobile clothing, boots, and gloves or mittens that still allow proper thumb and finger control of the throttle and brake.
<PIC>

9. Carry basic tools, spares, and emergency items
Bring a tool kit, flashlight, plastic tape, steel wire, tow rope, emergency starter rope, spare drive belt, spare bulbs, and spark plugs. For longer trips, carry extra fuel and oil, and ride with others whenever possible.

10. Final safety check before moving
Make sure no one is standing behind the snowmobile when starting or checking the track area. Confirm the engine stop switch works, the parking brake is released, the route is suitable for snow/ice operation, and the area ahead is clear before you begin riding.,['snowmobile_01', 'Manual34_97', 'Manual34_93', 'Manual34_96', 'Manual34_98', 'Manual34_11']

Here are the detailed steps to start the snowmobile engine. The process is slightly different depending on whether your model uses a recoil/manual starter or an electric starter.

Before starting, make these safety checks:
1. Make sure the parking brake is applied.
2. Check that the throttle lever moves freely and returns to idle when released.
3. Check that the brake and steering operate normally.
4. Make sure no one is standing behind the snowmobile, as the track area can be dangerous during starting or inspection.
5. Start only in a well-ventilated area. Do not run the engine in an enclosed space.
6. If your model has a drive select lever, confirm the lever position before moving the snowmobile.

General starting preparation:
1. Set the engine stop switch to the “ON” position.
2. Set the starter lever/choke according to engine temperature:
   - Fully open: for starting a cold engine.
   - Half-open: for warming up after the engine has started.
   - Close: for starting a warm engine or after warm-up is complete.

For a manual/recoil starting model:
1. Turn the main switch to the “ON” position.
2. Hold the starter handle firmly.
3. Pull the recoil starter slowly until you feel it engage.
4. Once engaged, pull it briskly and smoothly to start the engine. The image below shows the correct pulling action and hand position for the recoil starter.

<PIC>

5. After the engine starts, move the starter lever/choke to the “Half-open” position.
6. Let the engine warm up. During warm-up, the engine should gradually run smoothly.
7. When the engine no longer runs roughly or tries to stall, return the starter lever/choke to the “Close” position.
8. Do not ride away until the engine has warmed up properly, and remember to release the parking brake before moving.

For an electric starting model:
1. Turn the main switch to the “START” position. The image below shows the switch being turned to the START position.

<PIC>

2. As soon as the engine starts, release the switch immediately.
3. Move the starter lever/choke to the “Half-open” position.
4. Warm up the engine until it runs smoothly and does not begin to stall when the starter lever/choke is moved to the “Close” position.
5. After warm-up, return the starter lever/choke to the “Close” position.
6. Release the parking brake only when you are ready to move off.

Important electric-start caution:
- Do not keep the switch in the START position after the engine starts.
- If the engine does not start, release the switch, wait a few seconds, and try again.
- Keep each starting attempt as short as possible to protect the battery.
- Do not crank the engine for more than 10 seconds in a single attempt.,['Manual34_116', 'Manual34_117']

A V-Belt Holder is the dedicated bracket/holder on the machine used to carry a spare V-belt. Its purpose is to keep a replacement belt available on the vehicle for emergency use, so if the installed drive belt becomes worn, damaged, or fails during operation, you have a spare belt stored securely on the machine.

To access or remove the spare V-belt from the holder, loosen the bolt marked ①. This bolt secures the belt in the holder and prevents it from moving while the machine is in use.

<PIC>

When placing the spare V-belt back into the holder, make sure the belt is seated properly and then tighten the bolt firmly. Also check that nearby hoses, especially the fuel hose, are not pinched or displaced during installation.

<PIC>

Important: the V-belt must be secured tightly in the holder. If it comes loose, it may contact hot parts such as the muffler, which could damage the belt and make it unusable when you need it.,['Manual34_51', 'Manual34_52']

Before adjusting the throttle cable, make sure the engine idle speed has already been adjusted correctly. This is important because throttle cable free play is set relative to the correct idle condition.

1. Locate the throttle cable adjuster area and loosen the adjuster locknut.

2. Turn the adjuster in or out until the throttle lever free play is within the correct specification:
   - BR250T / ET410TR / VK540E: 1.0–2.0 mm (0.04–0.08 in)
   - CS340E: 0.5–1.0 mm (0.02–0.04 in)

<PIC>

In the adjustment diagrams:
- ① = throttle lever/cable free play
- ② = locknut
- ③ = adjuster

<PIC>

3. After the free play is set correctly, tighten the locknut securely while holding the adjuster so the setting does not change.

4. Recheck the throttle lever operation after tightening. The throttle should move smoothly and return fully when released. Also confirm the free play is still within the specified range.

<PIC>

If the throttle does not return smoothly, the free play cannot be set correctly, or the engine speed changes unexpectedly after adjustment, stop using the snowmobile and have the throttle system inspected before riding.,['Manual34_148', 'Manual34_149', 'Manual34_150']

The proper way to use the steering system is to combine correct handlebar control with body positioning, speed control, and a pre-ride steering check.

1. Check the steering before starting
Before you start the engine, confirm that the throttle, brake, and steering all operate properly. For the steering system specifically:
- Turn the handlebars left and right to make sure they move smoothly.
- Check that the skis respond correctly to handlebar movement.
- Make sure there is no excessive looseness, binding, or unusual movement in the handlebars, steering column, or skis.
- If the steering feels loose, stiff, uneven, or abnormal, do not ride until it has been inspected and corrected.

<PIC>

2. Keep proper riding posture
While riding, always keep both hands on the handlebars so you can control direction and react quickly. Keep your feet on the running boards at all times; do not place your feet outside the running boards while the snowmobile is moving.

3. Turn smoothly and use your body weight
On most snow surfaces, steering is not done by the handlebars alone. Snowmobiles require active body movement for stable turning.

When approaching a curve:
- Slow down before entering the turn.
- Begin turning the handlebars in the direction you want to go.
- Shift your weight onto the running board on the inside of the turn.
- Lean your upper body into the turn.

This body-weight technique helps the skis bite into the snow and keeps the machine balanced through the curve.

<PIC>

4. Adjust your lean for speed and turn sharpness
Practice turning at low speed in a large, flat, open area with no obstacles. As your skill improves, you can progress to tighter turns or slightly higher speeds. The sharper the turn or the higher the speed, the more you need to lean into the turn. Avoid abrupt throttle changes, excessive braking, or entering a turn too fast, as these can cause the snowmobile to tip or lose control.

5. If the snowmobile starts to tip
If the machine begins to tip while turning:
- Lean farther into the turn to help regain balance.
- If needed, gradually release the throttle.
- You may also steer slightly toward the outside of the turn to stabilize the machine.
Do not make sudden steering, braking, or throttle movements.

6. Be extra cautious on ice
On ice or icy surfaces, steering response is greatly reduced because the skis and track have much less traction. Ride slowly and cautiously, and avoid rapid acceleration, sharp turning, or sudden braking. Uncontrolled spins can occur easily on icy surfaces.

In short: check the steering before riding, keep both hands on the handlebars, steer smoothly, slow down before turns, and use body weight together with handlebar input for safe and effective control.,['Manual34_108', 'Manual34_127']

To turn a snowmobile correctly, use a combination of steering input, speed control, and body weight shift—often called “body English.” Follow these steps:

1. **Slow down before the curve**  
   As you approach the turn, reduce your speed early. Avoid entering the turn too fast, especially on hard-packed snow or icy surfaces where traction is lower.

2. **Turn the handlebars in the direction you want to go**  
   Begin steering smoothly toward the desired direction. Do not jerk the handlebars or make sudden steering movements.

3. **Shift your weight to the inside of the turn**  
   Put more weight on the running board on the inside of the turn. For example, if turning left, shift weight onto the left running board.

4. **Lean your upper body into the turn**  
   Move your upper body toward the inside of the curve to help the snowmobile stay balanced and track through the turn.

<PIC>

5. **Match your lean to the turn**  
   The sharper the turn or the higher the speed, the more you need to lean into the turn. However, avoid high-speed turns until you are fully comfortable with the snowmobile’s handling.

6. **Use smooth throttle and braking**  
   Keep throttle changes gradual. Avoid sudden acceleration or hard braking while turning, as this can reduce traction and may cause the snowmobile to tip or slide.

7. **If the snowmobile starts to tip**  
   Lean farther into the turn to regain balance. If needed, gradually ease off the throttle and/or steer slightly toward the outside of the turn to stabilize the machine.

For best safety, practice turns repeatedly at low speed in a large, flat, open area with no obstacles or traffic before attempting tighter turns, higher speeds, slopes, ice, or hard-packed snow.,['Manual34_127']

When riding uphill on a snowmobile, the key is to build momentum before the climb, manage traction carefully, and use your body position to keep the machine balanced and controlled.

1. Start with easier hills first
Practice on gentle slopes before attempting steeper or longer climbs. Uphill riding depends heavily on balance, throttle control, and reading the terrain, so build skill gradually before moving to more difficult hills.

2. Prepare before the climb
As you approach the hill, look ahead and choose the cleanest, safest line. Check for uneven snow, trees, rocks, hidden obstacles, or other riders. Gain enough speed before the slope begins, rather than trying to accelerate hard once you are already climbing.

3. Use smooth throttle control
Accelerate before starting the climb, then slightly reduce and steady the throttle as you go uphill. This helps prevent the track from spinning. If you apply too much throttle on the slope, the track may slip, reducing traction and making the snowmobile harder to control.

4. Keep your weight on the uphill side
Your body position is very important. When riding straight up a hill, lean forward to keep weight over the skis and help maintain steering control. On steeper inclines, stand on the running boards and lean forward over the handlebars. This forward position helps keep the front end down and improves stability.

<PIC>

5. Slow down near the crest
As you reach the top of the hill, reduce speed and be ready to react. There may be sharp drops, obstacles, other riders, vehicles, or unexpected terrain on the other side. Never charge blindly over the crest.

6. If you cannot continue uphill
If the snowmobile loses momentum and cannot keep climbing, do not keep spinning the track. Spinning can dig the machine in deeper and reduce control. Instead:
- Stop the engine.
- Set the parking brake.
- Turn the rear of the snowmobile around so the machine points back downhill.
- Stay on the uphill side of the snowmobile; do not move to the downhill side.
- Once it is pointed downhill, restart the engine, release the parking brake, and descend carefully.

7. Important safety note
Side hills and steep slopes are not recommended for beginners or novice riders. If you are still learning, stay on moderate terrain and avoid slopes where you cannot safely stop, turn around, or descend.,['Manual34_127']

When riding downhill on a snowmobile, the main goal is to stay controlled and prevent the machine from free-rolling or sliding. Use the following technique:

1. Keep your speed very low before and during the descent. Do not wait until you are already moving fast to slow down.

2. Maintain a small amount of throttle—just enough to keep the clutch engaged. This helps the engine compression slow the snowmobile and prevents it from rolling freely down the hill.

3. Use the brake lightly and repeatedly rather than grabbing it suddenly. Apply gentle pressure as needed to control speed.

4. Keep your body balanced and ready to adjust. Stay stable on the running boards and avoid abrupt body movements, steering inputs, or throttle changes.

5. Look ahead down the slope and plan your line early. Watch for bumps, hidden obstacles, icy patches, other riders, or sudden changes in terrain.

<PIC>

Important safety warning: Be especially careful when braking while descending. Excessive or sudden braking can lock the track, reduce traction, and cause loss of control. On icy or hard-packed surfaces, stopping distances can be much longer, so begin slowing early and ride cautiously.,['Manual34_128']

Crossing a slope, or side-hilling, requires careful body positioning and smooth control. It is not recommended for beginner or novice snowmobile riders, especially on steep, icy, or uneven slopes.

1. Prepare before crossing
- Choose the safest line across the slope and look ahead for rocks, trees, drop-offs, hidden obstacles, or other riders.
- Keep your speed low and steady. Avoid sudden acceleration, sharp steering, or hard braking.
- Keep both hands on the handlebars and your feet positioned securely so you can shift your weight quickly.

2. Shift your weight to the uphill side
As you travel across the face of the slope, lean your body toward the uphill side. This helps counter the pull of gravity and keeps the snowmobile balanced.

A recommended position is:
- Place the knee of your downhill leg on the seat.
- Keep the foot of your uphill leg on the uphill running board.
- Lean your upper body uphill so your weight stays on the high side of the snowmobile.

<PIC>

3. Keep control smooth and gradual
While crossing the slope:
- Maintain a controlled, moderate speed.
- Use gentle throttle input to avoid track spin.
- Avoid sudden braking, because it can reduce traction and cause sliding.
- Make small steering corrections rather than abrupt turns.

4. If the snowmobile begins to slide sideways
Snow and ice can cause the machine to slip downhill. If it starts sliding and there are no obstacles in your path, steer in the direction of the slide. Once balance and traction return, gradually steer back toward your intended direction of travel.

5. If the snowmobile starts to tip
If the snowmobile begins to tip, steer down the hill to help regain balance. Do this smoothly and avoid panicked braking or sharp movements.

6. If you cannot regain balance
If the snowmobile is going to tip over and you cannot correct it, dismount immediately on the uphill side. Never step or jump to the downhill side, as the machine could roll or slide toward you.

In short: cross slowly, lean uphill, keep your movements smooth, be ready for sliding, and always exit to the uphill side if the snowmobile is about to tip.,['Manual34_129']

To inspect the spark plug on a snowmobile, follow these steps:

1. Prepare the snowmobile safely
   - Stop the engine and let it cool completely before touching the engine area.
   - Keep the parking brake applied and make sure the machine is stable.
   - Avoid touching the engine or muffler while they are hot.

2. Remove the spark plug for inspection
   - Carefully pull off the spark plug cap.
   - Remove the spark plug using the proper spark plug wrench.
   - Inspect it periodically, because heat, deposits, and normal use can slowly erode or break down the plug.

3. Check the plug color and condition
   - Look at the white porcelain insulator around the center electrode.
   - A normal spark plug should be medium to light tan if the snowmobile is being ridden under normal conditions.
   - If the color is very different, it may indicate an engine or fuel/air mixture issue. For example, a very white insulator may point to an intake air leak or carburetion problem.
   - If the plug is wet, carbon-fouled, badly worn, cracked, or damaged, clean it if appropriate or replace it.

4. Confirm the correct spark plug reach
   Spark plug “reach” means the threaded length from the gasket seat to the end of the threaded portion. Using the wrong reach can cause problems:
   - Too long: may cause overheating and engine damage.
   - Too short: may cause fouling, poor performance, carbon buildup, hot spots, and thread damage.

   Use the correct reach for your model:
   - BR250T: 12.7 mm / 0.50 in
   - CS340E, ET410TR, VK540E: 19.0 mm / 0.75 in

<PIC>

5. Measure and adjust the electrode gap
   - Before reinstalling the spark plug, measure the electrode gap using a wire thickness gauge.
   - Adjust the gap to the required specification for your snowmobile.
   - In the illustration below, item 1 shows the spark plug reach, and item 2 shows the electrode gap to be measured.

<PIC>

6. Clean before installation
   - Clean the gasket seating surface.
   - Wipe dirt, oil, carbon, or grime from the spark plug threads.
   - Make sure the plug is not cross-threaded when reinstalling it.

7. Reinstall and tighten properly
   - Install the spark plug by hand first to avoid damaging the threads.
   - Then tighten it to the proper torque.

   Spark plug tightening torque: 28 Nm / 2.8 m-kg / 20 ft-lb

If the spark plug repeatedly becomes wet, carbon-fouled, unusually white, or the engine has low power, misfires, backfires, or is hard to start, the spark plug may not be the only issue. In that case, have the snowmobile checked by a qualified dealer or technician.,['Manual34_138', 'Manual34_139']

Here are the recommended steps to clean a snowmobile:

1. Prepare the snowmobile safely
   - Park it on a flat, stable surface.
   - Stop the engine and let it cool completely before cleaning.
   - Apply the parking brake.
   - Clean in a well-ventilated area and avoid spraying water directly into the exhaust outlet, fuel tank opening, electrical connectors, switches, or air intake areas.

2. Remove loose snow, ice, and debris
   - Brush off packed snow, ice, dirt, leaves, and trail debris from the body, skis, track area, suspension, running boards, and underbody.
   - Pay special attention to areas where road salt or dirty slush can collect, since salts and acids can cause corrosion.

3. Loosen mud, grease, and grime
   - Apply Mud and Grease Release, or an equivalent cleaner, to greasy or heavily soiled areas.
   - Let the cleaner work briefly, then gently loosen buildup with a soft brush or cloth.
   - Avoid harsh scraping tools that could damage painted, plastic, rubber, or finished metal surfaces.

4. Wash with mild soap
   - Use mild soap mixed with clean water.
   - Wash the exterior panels, tunnel, running boards, skis, suspension parts, and accessible track area.
   - Do not use aggressive chemicals, strong solvents, or high-pressure spray too close to seals, bearings, electrical parts, or decals.

5. Rinse completely
   - Rinse all soap and cleaner residue thoroughly with clean water.
   - Make sure no cleaner remains on metal suspension parts, the track area, or around fasteners, as residue can attract dirt or contribute to corrosion.

6. Dry completely
   - Wipe the snowmobile dry with clean towels or allow it to air-dry fully.
   - Dry around the seat, controls, running boards, suspension, and track area as much as possible.
   - If the snowmobile was exposed to road salt during transport, cleaning and drying it promptly is especially important.

7. Final check after cleaning
   - Check that the throttle, brake, and steering controls move freely.
   - Look over the track, skis, slide runners, and suspension for visible damage or unusual wear.
   - Before riding again, make sure the machine is fully dry and all controls operate normally.

The best way to memorize communication/TV channels with Manual Program depends on whether you want the quickest method or more control. You can use either of the following methods:

**Method 1: Quick memorizing with the MEMORY/ERASE button**  
This is the fastest option if you already know the channel number you want to add or remove.

1. Use the **▲/▼ Channel buttons** or the **NUMBER buttons** to go to the channel you want to store or delete.
2. Press the **MEMORY/ERASE** button.
3. Choose one of the following:
   - **Memory**: adds the channel to the stored channel list.
   - **Erase**: removes the channel from the stored channel list, so it will be skipped when using ▲/▼ channel selection.

<PIC>

**Method 2: Memorizing channels through the MENU**  
This method is best if you want to confirm the channel and memory setting from the on-screen menu.

1. Press the **MENU** button.
2. Use the **▲/▼ buttons** until the **CH.** channel menu appears.
3. Press the **▶ button** to enter the channel menu.
4. Use **▲/▼** to select **Manual**, then press **▶**.
5. Select **Channel**, then press **▶**.
6. Use **▲/▼** to choose the channel number you want to memorize or erase.

<PIC>

7. Move to **Memory**, then press **▶**.
8. Use **▲/▼** to select:
   - **On**: memorizes the selected channel.
   - **Off**: erases/skips the selected channel.
9. Press **MENU** repeatedly to exit.

<PIC>

**Recommendation:**  
Use the **MEMORY/ERASE button** for quick add/delete operation. Use the **MENU method** when you want a clearer step-by-step on-screen confirmation, especially if you are adjusting several channels one by one.,['Manual35_9', 'Manual35_10', 'Manual35_11']

Poor television or radio reception is usually caused by weak signal strength, signal interference, signal reflections, or antenna/cable issues. The most common causes and conditions are:

1. Electrical or ignition interference
This can make a TV picture flutter, drift, show black spots, or display horizontal streaks. For radio, it may sound like buzzing, crackling, popping, or intermittent noise. Common sources include automobile ignition systems, neon lamps, electrical drills, motors, fluorescent lights, and other electrical appliances operating nearby.
<PIC>

2. Signal reflections / ghosting
Ghosting happens when a TV signal reaches the receiver by more than one path: one direct signal and another reflected signal from tall buildings, hills, large metal structures, or other objects. This can create multiple images or a shadow-like duplicate on the screen. For radio, similar multipath reflections can cause fading, distortion, or wavering audio. Repositioning or redirecting the antenna often helps.
<PIC>

3. Weak signal or fringe-area reception
If the TV or radio is far from the broadcast transmitter, blocked by terrain, or located in a weak-signal area, the received signal may be too low. On TV, this can appear as “snow,” small dots, unstable picture, or missing channels. On radio, it may cause hiss, fading, or difficulty locking onto a station. In these conditions, a better-positioned antenna, an outdoor antenna, or a more suitable antenna type may be needed.
<PIC>

4. Antenna direction, position, or connection problems
Poor reception can result from a loose antenna plug, damaged coaxial cable, incorrect antenna connection, or an antenna pointed in the wrong direction. Severely weathered outdoor antennas and antenna cables can also reduce signal quality. Make sure the antenna or CATV cable is firmly connected to the 75-ohm antenna jack, and inspect outdoor cables for corrosion, cracks, or looseness.

5. Local interference from vehicles, aircraft, or nearby equipment
Strong random signals from cars, aircraft, or nearby electrical devices can interfere with TV or radio signals. This type of interference may come and go depending on traffic, aircraft movement, or when certain appliances are switched on.

6. Obstructions and surrounding environment
Buildings, hills, trees, large metal furniture, metal roofing, and indoor placement can all reduce or reflect signals. For radio, reception may also worsen inside basements, elevators, reinforced concrete buildings, or near large metal objects. Moving the receiver or antenna closer to a window or higher location can improve reception.

7. Weather and atmospheric conditions
Heavy rain, storms, strong winds, lightning activity, and certain atmospheric conditions can temporarily weaken or distort broadcast signals. Outdoor antennas may also shift position during wind, which can reduce reception quality.

8. Broadcast or channel availability limitations
Sometimes the issue is not with the TV or radio but with the station signal itself. A station may be weak in your area, temporarily off-air, transmitting in mono only, or not broadcasting certain services. For TV, if a channel has poor signal quality, running AUTO PROGRAM again may help the TV store the available receivable channels correctly.

Recommended checks:
- Try another channel or station to confirm whether the issue affects only one broadcast.
- Check that the antenna or cable connection is secure.
- Move or rotate the antenna and test reception again.
- Keep the TV/radio and antenna away from strong electrical appliances or motors.
- Inspect outdoor antennas and cables for weather damage.
- For TV channels, run AUTO PROGRAM again if channels are missing or signal quality has changed.
- If the picture has horizontal stripes, twisted images, or unstable color on a specific TV channel, fine tuning that channel may help.
<PIC>,['television0_01', 'television0_02', 'television0_03', 'Manual35_45']

Closed captions display the dialogue and narration of a TV program as written text on the screen, similar to subtitles. The TV also supports text modes, which can display full-screen information such as program lists, weather, news, or other broadcast text services when provided by the TV station.

<PIC>

You can turn captions or on-screen text on and select the desired mode in either of the following ways:

**Option 1: Use the CAPTION button on the remote**

1. Press the **CAPTION** button on the remote control.
2. Press **CAPTION** repeatedly to cycle through the available settings:
   - **OFF**: Captions/text are turned off.
   - **Mode1**: Usually the main closed-caption signal for program dialogue.
   - **Mode2**: May provide a secondary caption signal, such as alternate or program-related information.
   - **Text1 / Text2**: Displays text-service information across the screen when the broadcast provides it.
3. When the setting you want is displayed, press **ENTER (O)** to exit.

<PIC>

**Option 2: Use the on-screen MENU**

1. Press the **MENU** button.
2. Use the **up/down** buttons to select the **Special** menu.
3. Press the **right** button to enter the Special menu.
4. Use the **up/down** buttons to select **Caption**.
5. Press the **right** button, then use the **up/down** buttons to choose one of the following: **Off, Mode1, Mode2, Text1, or Text2**.
6. Press **MENU** repeatedly to close the menu.

<PIC>

**Helpful notes:**

- The TV remembers the last caption/text mode you selected, even after the TV is turned off.
- Not every broadcast includes caption or text signals. If captions do not appear, try another channel or program.
- **Mode1** is the setting most commonly used for standard captions.
- **Mode2** may show secondary caption information if the broadcaster provides it.
- **Text1/Text2** may show a blank or black screen area if the station does not provide text service or if the signal is weak.
- Poor reception, antenna issues, interference, or programs without caption data can prevent captions from displaying correctly.,['Manual35_36', 'Manual35_37', 'Manual35_38']

For optimal outdoor antenna reception, please connect the antenna securely, scan for available channels, and then adjust the antenna position if the signal is weak or distorted.

1. Check and prepare the outdoor antenna

Before connecting, inspect the outdoor antenna and cable carefully:
- Make sure the antenna is not badly weathered, bent, rusted, or loose.
- Check that the cable jacket is not cracked, cut, or water-damaged.
- Use a firm, stable mounting location outdoors, preferably higher and with a clear line of sight.
- Keep the antenna and cable away from strong electrical interference sources where possible.
- For outdoor installation, ensure the antenna is securely fixed and the cable connection is protected from rain and moisture. If the antenna is mounted high, near power lines, or requires grounding/lightning protection, we recommend having it installed by a qualified professional for safety.

2. Connect the antenna cable to the TV

On the rear of the TV, locate the 75 ohm antenna jack.

If you are using 300 ohm flat wire:
1. Connect the two ends of the 300 ohm flat wire to the screws on a 300 ohm to 75 ohm adapter.
2. Tighten the screws so the wire is held firmly.
3. Insert the adapter into the TV’s 75 ohm antenna jack.

If you are using a 75 ohm coaxial cable:
1. Connect the coaxial cable directly to the TV’s 75 ohm antenna jack.
2. Make sure the connector is fully inserted and secure.

<PIC>

3. Turn on the TV and run Auto Program

After the antenna is connected, turn on the TV and store the available broadcast channels.

Using the AUTO PRG button:
1. Press the AUTO PRG. button on the remote control.
2. Press the AUTO PRG. button again, or press the right/next button when prompted, to start Auto Program.
3. The TV will search and memorize receivable channels in your area.
4. If you need to stop the scan before it finishes, press ENTER. Channels found up to that point will be saved.

<PIC>

During scanning, the TV will show the current channel and progress percentage. Please wait until the search is complete for the best result.

<PIC>

4. Improve reception by adjusting the antenna

If some channels are missing or the picture quality is poor, adjust the antenna and scan again.

For ghosting or double images:
- This is often caused by the signal reflecting off buildings, hills, or other large objects.
- Slowly rotate or reposition the outdoor antenna.
- Raise the antenna or move it to a more open location if possible.
- After changing the antenna position, run Auto Program again.

<PIC>

For snowy or dotted pictures:
- This usually means the signal is weak.
- Check that the antenna cable is connected firmly.
- Try raising the antenna, using a better outdoor antenna, or pointing it more accurately toward the broadcast source.
- If you are in a weak-signal area, a higher-gain outdoor antenna may be needed.

<PIC>

5. Fine-tune individual channels if needed

If only one or a few channels still show issues such as horizontal stripes, a twisted picture, or no color, use Fine adjustment:
1. Press MENU.
2. Select the CH. menu.
3. Select Manual.
4. Select Fine.
5. Use the left/right buttons to adjust until the picture becomes stable.
6. Press ENTER to save the adjustment.
7. Press MENU repeatedly to exit.

<PIC>

If a channel becomes worse after fine-tuning, run Auto Program again or adjust the channel manually to restore normal tuning.,['Manual35_39', 'Manual35_5', 'Manual35_7', 'television0_02', 'television0_03', 'Manual35_24']

To get the best performance when connecting a DVD player, use the highest-quality video connection available on your TV and connect the audio either to the TV or to an external audio system.

1. Turn off all equipment first

Before connecting cables, turn off the TV, DVD player, and any audio receiver/amplifier. This helps prevent connection noise and protects the ports.

2. Connect the DVD player to the TV using component video

If your TV has COMPONENT DVD INPUT jacks, connect the DVD player’s component video outputs to the TV’s component inputs:

- DVD Y output → TV Y input
- DVD Pb / Cb / B-Y / PB output → TV PB input
- DVD Pr / Cr / R-Y / PR output → TV PR input

These three cables carry the video signal only. For this TV, the component DVD input supports 480i.

<PIC>

3. Connect the audio

Choose one of the following audio connection methods:

Option A: Audio through the TV

Connect the DVD player’s AUDIO OUT jacks to the TV’s AUDIO IN jacks beside the component inputs:

- DVD L audio output → TV L audio input
- DVD R audio output → TV R audio input

This allows the DVD sound to play through the TV speakers.

Option B: Audio through an external audio system

For better sound, connect the DVD player directly to your audio receiver, amplifier, or speaker system:

- Connect DVD AUDIO OUT L/R to the audio system’s matching AUDIO IN L/R jacks.
- Match white to L/Left and red to R/Right.
- Then select the correct input on the audio system, such as DVD, AUX, or LINE IN.

If your DVD player and audio system support a digital audio connection, you may use that instead for improved surround-sound performance. In that case, select the matching digital input on the audio system.

4. Check the component terminal names carefully

Different DVD players may label the component outputs differently. They still connect to the same TV inputs as follows:

- Y connects to Y
- Cb / B-Y / Pb / PB connects to PB
- Cr / R-Y / Pr / PR connects to PR

<PIC>

5. Turn on the devices in the correct order

After the cables are connected:

1. Turn on the TV.
2. Turn on the DVD player.
3. If using an external audio system, turn it on and select the correct audio input.

6. Select the correct TV input

Press the TV/VIDEO button on the TV remote control or on the TV front panel until COMPONENT is selected. The DVD picture should then appear on the screen.

7. Start playback and fine-tune settings

Insert a DVD and press PLAY on the DVD player. If you see video but do not hear sound, recheck the L/R audio cables or confirm that the audio system is on the correct input. If there is sound but no picture, confirm that the Y, PB, and PR cables are connected to the matching ports and that the TV is set to COMPONENT.

Please note: The TV remembers the last selected input mode even after it is turned off, so it may return to COMPONENT automatically the next time you use it.,['Manual35_43', 'Manual35_44']

We are very sorry for the inconvenience caused by the technical issue you are experiencing and we will do our best to help you resolve it. To troubleshoot effectively, please first verify that the power source is stable and all cables or batteries are correctly installed and connected.
